segments.s236-g145: شما (you) is the polite/second‑person plural subject. سربسر is an adverb meaning 'completely/altogether' modifying the verb phrase. بهره دارید is a present tense possession construction: بهره (a 'share' or 'portion') + دارید (present 2nd‑person plural of داشتن 'to have') = 'you have a share'. زین here points to 'this' (literally 'of/with this [saddle/trappings]' in poetic usage) and attaches as the object/thing possessed.
segments.s236-g146: نه جای گله‌ست is a negated nominal clause: نه (not) + جای (place) + گله‌ست (گله + است, 'it is a complaint') = 'this is no place for complaint'. از introduces the target/agent of the complaint ('about, concerning'), and جهان‌آفرین is a compound noun 'world‑creator' (God); together 'not a place to complain about the Creator.'
segments.s236-g147: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb meaning 'he commanded/said' (classical usage where 'to say' covers issuing an order). تا here functions as the complementizer 'that'/'so that' introducing the content of the command. گیو is the proper name (Giv). با is the preposition 'with', introducing the accompanying force (ده هزار follows implicitly as a numeral phrase describing those 'with' Giv).
segments.s236-g148: سپردار here names the fighters — in epic usage it can mean 'armed men/warriors' (not literally 'commanders' given the numeral). و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. بر is the preposition 'on/upon' often used in poetic descriptions of being mounted. گستوان is a rare/poetic adjective/noun (textually obscure) modifying سوار; the sequence ور سوار is a compound descriptor of the horsemen. Altogether the phrase describes mounted fighters (literally 'on broad/— [gostavān] and ... mounted').
segments.s236-g149: شود is the subjunctive/presentive of شدن used to express a wish/let (here 'let/may ... go'). تازیان (the Arabs) is the subject. تا introduces the extent 'as far as'; بمرز is the prepositional phrase 'to the border (to the frontier)'. ختن here refers to the place name Khotan (appearing in the text as a locality), so the whole phrase means 'as far as the border of Khotan.'
segments.s236-g150: نماند is a negative/resultative verb 'remain/ be left' with negation implied by the context = 'there will not remain/there will be no ... left'. که introduces a subordinate clause ('that/so that'). ترکان is the subject 'the Turks'. شوند is the subjunctive/passive form of شدن here meaning 'become' or more idiomatically 'will be' or 'to hold' in 'become an assembly'. انجمن is the object 'assembly/gathering'. Together: 'so that the Turks will have no assembly left.'
segments.s236-g151: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بنمود is a past tense verb 'appeared/revealed' (poetic past of نمودن). شب (night) here is personified and modified by the apposition جعد زلف سیاه = 'curly black locks' (جعد 'curls', زلف 'locks of hair', سیاه 'black'), a frequent poetic image where night is likened to a dark-haired lover; the noun phrase is the subject of بنمود.
segments.s236-g152: از + اندیشه indicates cause or source here ('from/because of thought' i.e. 'with worry/thinking'). خمیده شد is a compound verb: خمیده (bent) + شد (became) = 'became bent'. پشت ماه is a genitive phrase 'the back/brow of the moon' (a common poetic expression); together the clause says the moon's brow was bent because of thought/worry.
segments.s236-g129: ز + noun + گشتن is a poetic prepositional/temporal/causal phrase meaning 'upon/after becoming...'. پیروز گشتن is a verbal phrase 'to become victorious,' and نیایش گرفت (گرفت used transitively) is a literary way to say 'he took up/entered into prayer' or 'he offered a prayer.' Word order is literary: the temporal/causal clause (ز پیروز گشتن) comes first and is followed by the action.
segments.s236-g130: جهان‌آفرین را is the object marked by را (definite object marker). ستایش گرفت uses گرفت again in the sense 'took up/began' and together means 'he offered/poured out praise' (i.e. 'he praised'). The clause parallels the previous one (first prayer, then praise).
segments.s236-g131: بایرانیان is the plural subject 'the Iranians.' گفت here functions as past 'they spoke/they said' (literary register). با + کردگار indicates they addressed or spoke 'with/to the Creator/God' — the preposition با is used in the older/poetic diction where Modern Persian might use به.
segments.s236-g132: بیامد is a past tense verb 'they came.' نهانی هم از آشکار is an adverbial phrase meaning 'both secretly and from the open (i.e. openly)' — هم marks 'also/both' and از آشکار ('from the manifest') yields 'openly.' Together the phrase qualifies the coming: they came sometimes secretly, sometimes openly (or both secretly and openly).
segments.s236-g133: بپیروزی is a prepositional/adverbial formation (بـ + پیروزی) meaning 'upon/at/for victory.' اندر is a locative preposition 'in/within.' نیایش کنید is a plural/probably polite imperative/subjunctive 'perform/offer prayer' (کنید is the present subjunctive/imperative form used as a command in plural or polite address). The clause is an exhortation: 'in victory, pray.'
segments.s236-g134: جهان‌آفرین را is the definite direct object 'the Creator' marked by را. ستایش کنید is the plural/polite imperative 'praise' (ستایش + کردن with کنید). The two clauses are parallel imperatives urging prayer and praise.
segments.s236-g135: بزرگان is the plural noun 'the great ones' or elders. بپیش is a preposition meaning 'before' in the sense 'in the presence of' (بـ + پیش), so بپیش جهان‌آفرین = 'before the Creator.' This phrase sets the scene: the dignitaries standing in God's presence.
segments.s236-g136: نیایش گرفتند is past plural 'they took/entered into prayer' (گرفتند used transitively as in earlier clauses). سر بر زمین is a fixed locative phrase 'head upon the ground' (سر = 'head', بر = 'on/upon', زمین = 'the ground'), describing the posture of prostration during prayer.
segments.s236-g465: This is a compact, poetic clause: 'بیابان سپردی' = 'you handed (him) over to the desert', where سپردی is a past-tense 2nd-person verb and the direct object is omitted (understood from context). The following phrase 'و راه دراز' is a coordinate noun phrase 'and a long road' with the adjective دراز placed after the noun راه (typical Persian word order, especially in poetry).
segments.s236-g466: 'کنون' is a temporal adverb 'now' introducing an imperative clause. 'چاره کار او را بساز' is an imperative: بساز (2nd-person singular imperative) 'make/devise'; 'چاره' 'solution' modifies 'کار' 'affair/case', and 'او را' (with the object marker را) marks 'his case' as the definite object placed before the verb for emphasis — a normal order in classical Persian poetry.
segments.s236-g467: The predicate 'پر اندیشه شد' literally 'became full of thought' uses پر as a preposed adjective/quantifier 'full (of)' + a noun اندیشه, with شد as the change-of-state verb 'became'. Poetic word order places this predicate before the subject 'جان پولادوند' (the soul/life of Pūlādvand), which is the entity experiencing the change.
segments.s236-g468: The conjunction/introducer 'که' begins a subordinate clause (an indirect question). 'آن بند را' is the definite object (marked by را) 'that bond/fetter'. 'چون شود' = 'how will it be / how it will become', where چون here functions as 'how' and شود is the classical/subjunctive/future-like form of 'become'. 'کاربند' is a predicate noun (result/state) meaning roughly 'a working fetter' or 'effective restraint'.
segments.s236-g469: 'چنین داد پاسخ بافراسیاب' is a simple past clause: چنین 'thus', داد 'gave' (past 3rd-person), پاسخ 'an answer' as the direct object placed before the verb, and بافراسیاب (با + name) marking the addressee 'to/for Afrasiab' — a dative/with-phrase common in classical usage.
segments.s236-g470: The particle 'که' introduces the content of the answer. 'در جنگ' is an adverbial prepositional phrase 'in battle', 'چندین' here functions adverbially 'much/too much' or 'many a', modifying the negative modal نباید 'one should not / must not', and شتاب is a verbal-noun 'haste' — together giving the counsel 'in battle one ought not to be hasty.'
segments.s236-g471: This conditional opening uses 'گر' (if) and the poetic fused copula 'آنست' (= آن است) 'it is that'; 'رستم' is the asserted identity. The relative particle 'که' begins a clause qualifying Rostam (continued in the next segment). Poetic inversion places the copula before the name.
segments.s236-g472: 'تبه کرد' is a past-tense verb phrase 'did ruin' (تبه used as a verbal noun/verb 'to ruin'); 'و بستد' continues with 'and he bound/closed' (بستد is past). The final noun phrase 'بگرز گران' is an instrument or object modified by the adjective گران 'heavy' (adjective follows noun in Persian poetry). Grammatically, the verb(s) precede or coordinate and the instrument/object follows; exact lexical nuance of بگرز depends on context (here treated as a concrete noun modified by گران).
segments.s236-g393: تبیره زنان نعره برداشتند is a nominal phrase (تبیره زنان = 'the Tabireh women') followed by a past-tense verb برداشتند 'they raised/uttered'. In classical Persian poetry برداشتند commonly collocates with نعره to mean 'gave a cry'. Word order is Subject (تبیره زنان) + Object/phrase (نعره) + Past verb (برداشتند).
segments.s236-g394: همی پیل بر پیل بگذاشتند uses همی (an archaic/frequentive adverb) plus a past plural verb بگذاشتند 'they placed', so the clause means 'they placed/stacked'. The repeated noun پیل بر پیل 'elephant upon elephant' is a poetic reduplication meaning rows/ranks of elephants; word order is adverb + (noun + postposition + noun) + verb.
segments.s236-g395: از ایوان بدشت آمد افراسیاب is a locative-motion clause: از + place_of_origin (ایوان) + destination (بدشت) + past verb آمد 'came', with the subject name (افراسیاب) at the end — a common Persian order where the verb precedes or follows the subject in poetry. بدشت here indicates 'to the plain'.
segments.s236-g396: همی کرد بر جنگ جستن شتاب contains the frequentive/continuous marker همی plus کرد as an auxiliary meaning 'made/began'; بر جنگ جستن = 'to go/seek to battle' (بر + noun + verbal infinitive جستن), and شتاب 'haste' is governed by کرد (i.e. 'he made haste'). Word-by-word: he 'did/made' haste in seeking battle.
segments.s236-g397: بپیران بگفت آنچ بایست گفت is 'he told the elders (بپیران بگفت) what ought to be said (آنچ بایست گفت)'. بپیران is a prepositional/oblique phrase 'to the elders', بگفت is the past-tense verb 'he said/told', and آنچ بایست گفت is a relative-like construction 'that which must be said' where بایست is an archaic modal 'ought/must'.
segments.s236-g398: که راز بزرگان بباید نهفت is a subordinate clause introduced by که 'that/because' stating obligation: راز بزرگان 'the secrets of the great' + بباید 'must/ought' + نهفت used as 'be hidden' (passive sense). The construction expresses necessity that those secrets remain concealed.
segments.s236-g399: یکی نامه نزدیک پولادوند is a noun phrase 'a certain/a single letter near Poladvand' with یکی functioning as an indefinite modifier 'a certain' before نامه; نزدِیک indicates proximity or addressing 'near/to Poladvand' (Poladvand is an epithet 'steel-clad'). Word order: determiner + noun + locative/adjective phrase.
segments.s236-g400: بیارای وز رای بگشای بند is an imperative or hortative cluster: بیارای (here archaic/poetic verb form meaning 'prepare/bring/arrange') followed by وز (a connective/particle) + رای 'counsel/plan' + بگشای 'open/release' + بند 'bond/fetter'. The sequence directs someone to prepare the letter and, by counsel, open or loosen the bond (i.e. free/unseal).
segments.s236-g361: سراپرده سبز (noun + adjective) = 'green inner‑chamber/tent'; دیدم is the past‑tense 1st‑person verb 'I saw'. The adjective بزرگ follows the verb here because it begins to attach to the next noun phrase (a common flexible word order in poetry); read together this segment means 'I saw a great green inner‑chamber.'
segments.s236-g362: This noun phrase lists qualities of a man: سپاهی (soldier/warrior) is the head noun; بکردار is an adjectival compound meaning 'of good conduct/well‑behaved' and modifies سپاهی; درنده is another adjective 'ferocious/predatory' that further describes him; گرگ is a simile/metaphor 'wolf' — together they mean 'a soldier of good bearing, ferocious, a wolf.' Note there is no copula in Persian poetry — adjectives follow the noun they modify.
segments.s236-g363: یکی = 'a certain' (introduces an individual); اژدهافش is a compound adjective 'dragon‑bannered' or 'dragon‑like' describing the banner/appearance; درفشی (here) = 'standard‑bearer' (the man who carries the banner); بپای is a verbal/adverbial phrase 'lying in wait / abiding there' (بـ + پای, poetic form). Together: 'a certain dragon‑bannered standard‑bearer lay in wait.'
segments.s236-g364: نه ... نه is the correlative negation 'neither ... nor'. آرام دارد = literally 'has calm/rest' (دارد is present‑tense 'has'); گفتی here functions as a noun 'speech/conversation' (poetic usage) so تو گفتی reads as 'a place for speaking/talking' (your/people's talk) and جای = 'place'. The sequence therefore says he 'has neither calm nor a place for speaking' — i.e. 'he is neither at rest nor has a place for speech.' This is a compact poetic phrasing rather than a literal address to 'you.'
segments.s236-g365: فروهشته is a past‑participle/adjectival form 'thrown down / scattered' placed before the locative phrase بر کوهه 'upon the hill'; زین لگام is a paired object 'saddle and bridle' (in poetry the conjunction is often omitted). Word order: adjective/predicate → locative → objects, yielding 'the saddle and bridle were thrown down on the hill.'
segments.s236-g366: بفتراک is adverbial/poetic meaning roughly 'apart / separated'; بر حلقه = 'on the ring' (حلقه = metal ring or loop); خم خام is a noun + adjective 'a bent (curve) that is raw/unseasoned' — the phrase describes how a strap or fitting hangs on the ring, 'hung apart on the ring, bent and raw.' This segment has no finite verb; it continues descriptive listing in poetic style.
segments.s236-g367: بخیمه درون = 'in the tent, inside' (بـ + خیمه with درون for emphasis 'within the tent'); ژنده is an adjective 'ragged' modifying the following noun phrase; پیلی here means 'sallow / elephantine' (physical description), and ژیان (literally 'life' or 'one living') is used poetically for 'creature/person' — together producing 'a ragged, sallow wretch' inside the tent. The order (adjectives before/after noun) is flexible in poetry.
segments.s236-g368: میان تنگ بسته = 'in the narrow, closed midst/space' (میان = 'in the middle of / among'); به is the preposition 'to/into' or a linker here 'in'/'within'; ببر is the noun 'tiger' used metaphorically; بیان is 'eloquence / expression'. The phrase 'به ببر بیان' is a poetic kenning 'the tiger of eloquence' (an eloquent/towering speaker) and the whole segment says this eloquent tiger was confined in the tight closed space. The construction is nominal and descriptive rather than a full clause with a separate verb.
segments.s236-g177: ستاره is the subject 'the star'. The cluster ب + دان functions poetically as a locative/ directional element ('to/at that'), so the phrase means 'watched over that plain'; نظاره is a noun 'watch/observation' and بود is the past copula 'was' setting the scene. Word order is subject — (prepositional locative) — noun of action — copula, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s236-g178: که here introduces a causal/explanatory clause 'for/because'. این لشکر is the nominal subject 'this army'; از + جنگ means 'because of the war/fighting' (از used causally), بیچاره is a predicate adjective 'unfortunate/helpless', and بود is the past copula 'was'. The clause explains why the star watched.
segments.s236-g179: بگشتیم is the past tense 'we returned/turned' (first-person plural). گرد + [دژ] gives the sense 'around the fortress'; ایدر is a locative adverb 'here'. بسی is an adverb meaning 'many/much' or 'often' and modifies the action 'we turned/rounded'. Word order places the verb first (common in older poetry) with locatives and adverbial qualifiers following.
segments.s236-g180: ندیدیم is the negative past 'we did not see' (negation prefix ن- on دیدیم). جز introduces an exception 'except/only', so 'ندیدیم جز …' = 'we saw nothing but …'. کینه is the noun 'hatred/grudge' and درمان is used as 'remedy/cure' (here: there was no cure). کسی is 'anyone' (no one to remedy it). The phrase is elliptical: 'we saw nothing but hatred — no remedy from anyone.'
segments.s236-g181: که again introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause 'for/that/when'. خوشان functions as a substantive 'they/them (the folk/people)' or 'the happy ones' in older diction; بدیم here is poetically terse and should be read as indicating misfortune/affliction (an older/poetic usage of a negative/bad sense), so the phrase with از دم اژدها reads 'when they were smitten by the dragon's breath'. از + دم اژدها is the ablative 'from the dragon's breath'.
segments.s236-g182: کمان تو is the topic/agent 'your bow'. آورد is the simple past 'brought/caused'; ما را is the object 'us' with the definite object marker را. رها is a predicate/adjectival/result 'free/released' that completes آورد (i.e., 'brought us free' = 'delivered us'). The construction is terse and paratactic (verb + object + result), common in epic poetry.
segments.s236-g183: توی used here is locative/attributive 'in/among' (older poetic form), and پشت in this idiom means 'support/back' — 'the support (or bulwark) of Iran'. و links the next noun phrase تاج سران 'the crown of the chiefs' (تاج = crown, سران = chiefs/leaders). The whole phrase attributes to the addressee the role of protector and emblem of leadership.
segments.s236-g184: سزاوار is a predicative adjective 'deserving/rightful' applying to the preceding titles. و 'and' links the contrasting statement: ما پیش تو 'we before you' (پیش = in the presence of/before) + کهتران (an older plural meaning 'the lesser/lowlier ones'). The clause states the speaker's humility in relation to the addressee. Word order places the predicate and contrastive conjunction after the titles for emphasis.
segments.s236-g297: کنون = adverb 'now/presently' placed at the clause start. هرچ is an indefinite pronoun 'whatever/whatever there is' and here introduces an enumerated list. گنجست is a contracted poetic form of گنج است ('is treasure' or 'there is treasure'); the verb/copula is compressed onto the noun. The coordinating و join the items 'تاج' (crown) and 'کمر' (here 'girdle/belt'), so the whole segment lists possessions 'whatever treasure there is: crown and girdle.'
segments.s236-g298: همان is a demonstrative 'that very / the same' pointing back to the previously listed treasures. طوق زرین = 'golden collar/necklace' (noun + adjective); Persian places the adjective after the noun. و links the next noun phrase زرین سپر 'golden shield'. The repetition of زرین (golden) is emphatic/poetic.
segments.s236-g299: فرستم is first-person singular present/optative 'I send / I will send' (in older Persian this form can carry a hortative/intentional sense). همه 'everyone/all' is the object, and سوی 'toward' governs the goal phrase الماس رود 'the Diamond River' (noun + noun). Word order is Subject-Verb-Object-Complement (with goal after سوی).
segments.s236-g300: نه is the negation introducing the clause 'it is not the time for...'. هنگام means 'time/occasion (for)'. جامست is a contracted poetic form of جام است ('it is the cup/time for drinking') — here treated as 'cup/drinking' and the contraction places the copula on the noun. و connects بزم 'feast/revel' and سرود 'song/ode'. The whole clause means 'this is not the time for cup, revel, and song.'
segments.s236-g301: هراسانم is an adjective هراسان 'frightened' with the first-person enclitic -م 'I am frightened' (predicate adjective working as a stative verb). از marks cause/agent 'of/from' and introduces the agent رستم (Rostam). تیز چنگ is a noun phrase 'sharp (=ti z) claw/grasp' with the adjective before the noun; چنگ here means 'claw/ta-lon' or 'grasp' (metaphorical for a powerful hand). The segment says 'I am terrified of Rostam's sharp grasp.'
segments.s236-g302: تن‌آسان is a compound adjective 'soft-bodied / luxury-loving' (literally 'body-easy'). که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'who/that' or a rhetorical question; باشد is third-person present/subjunctive of 'to be' (here optative/irrealis 'would be' or 'is/should be'); بکام is a prepositional/static construction meaning 'to the liking/pleasure (of)'. نهنگ 'whale' is used metaphorically. The clause asks rhetorically what value a soft-bodied man would have 'to the whale' (i.e., as prey).
segments.s236-g303: بمردم is an older/formal conditional/subjunctive form meaning 'if I die' (ب- prefix plus present-subjunctive-like form marks an irrealis/conditional). نماند 'will not remain / will be left no' is negative present/future-like 'there will be none left'. بروز combines preposition ب- with روز 'on the day' and here means 'on the day/for the day', and نبرد is 'battle'; together: 'if I die, there will be no day of battle left.'
segments.s236-g304: This is a pair of negated verbs linked by و. نپیچد (ن + پیچد) literally 'does not turn/retreat' — here idiomatically 'will not flinch/turn away' — followed by ز بیم 'from fear' (ز = 'from/because of'). ننالد (ن + نالد) 'does not moan/complain' followed by ز درد 'from pain'. Both verbs are in the present/future irrealis negative form and describe someone who will neither shrink from fear nor lament from pain.
segments.s236-g217: This clause starts with the vocative/pronoun تو ('you') followed by آنی, an older usage meaning 'the sort/one who' (here treated as a nominalizer), then the relative particle که introduces the relative clause. از خاک آوردگاه is a prepositional phrase 'from the dust/soil of the battlefield' (از + خاک + آوردگاه), with آوردگاه 'battlefield' as the head noun.
segments.s236-g218: همی is the archaic/progressive particle marking continuous or habitual action before the verbal idea. جوش خون is a nominalized image 'the surge/boiling of blood' (جوش modifying خون). اندر is a preposition 'within/inside'. The final phrase (آری بماه) is elliptical and poetic; همی + noun phrase gives the sense of 'blood is continually boiling inside (you)'.
segments.s236-g219: سلیحست is a fused form (سِلاح + است) meaning 'is armed' or 'is well‑armed'; classical Persian often attaches the copula. بسیار here qualifies the quantity ('many' — there are many), and و simply links the next noun phrase. مردان جنگ is a compound 'men/warriors of war' (مردان governed by جنگ).
segments.s236-g220: دل از کار رستم چه داری بتنگ is an interrogative: دل 'heart' + از کار رستم 'because of / about Rostam's deeds'. چه داری is the question 'what do you have' (literally 'what have you'), and بتنگ is a predicative adjective 'narrow/distressed' describing the heart. Overall: 'Why is your heart distressed about Rostam's deeds?'
segments.s236-g221: ز (poetic form of از) 'because of / from' + جنگ سواری 'the war's cavalry/riders' forms the reason clause. تو 'you' is the addressee. غمگین مشو is the negative imperative 'do not become sad' (مشو = negative of شدن). The phrase as a whole is an admonition: 'Do not be saddened by the cavalry of the battle.'
segments.s236-g222: نگه کن is an imperative 'look! / keep watch' (نگه = gaze/watch, کن = do!). بدین is the prepositional phrase 'at these' (به + این in running text) pointing to what to look at, followed by نامداران نو 'renowned/new men' — an instruction to regard these newly famed warriors. Note: the written separation of ب and دین is orthographic here; syntactically they form بدین ('to these').
segments.s236-g223: چنان دان is an imperative or hortative 'consider/think thus' (چنان = thus, دان = consider/know). The clause that follows is a simile/assertion: او یکسر از آهنست — او 'he', یکسر 'completely/entirely', از آهنست 'is of iron' (آهن + است, the copula suffixed). This is a figurative description 'think of him as made of iron.'
segments.s236-g224: اگر چه here functions idiomatically as 'although' (اگر + چه), introducing a concessive clause. دلیرست 'is brave' (دلیر + است). هم 'yet/still' reinforces the concession, and یک تنست (یک + تن + است) literally 'is one body' meaning 'is only one person' — the point being: even brave and iron‑like, he is only a single man.
segments.s236-g489: که is the clause-introducing conjunction 'that' (introducing what follows). من is the 1st-person subject 'I'. بر before a series of names functions here as a preposition 'against / toward' indicating the people affected (Fereydun, Zahhāk, Jam) — the three names are simply coordinated with و 'and'. The whole phrase sets up the persons who suffer the action expressed in the next segment.
segments.s236-g490: خور و خواب و آرام are three coordinated nouns 'food/eating, sleep, and rest/peace' forming the objects of کردم. کردم is the 1st‑person past of کردن 'I made/did/caused' and here is best understood as 'I deprived them of / I made them lose' (a common poetic meaning when listing comforts). دژم is an adjectival predicate 'grim/sullen' describing the speaker; placed after the verb for emphasis, it characterizes the speaker's mood while declaring the action.
segments.s236-g491: برهمن (Brāhmin) is the subject. بترسد is a 3rd‑person present/future form meaning 'will fear / fears' (the b- prefix is the classical present marker). ز is the poetic preposition 'from' or 'because of', and آواز من = 'my voice' (آواز governed by ز). The clause means 'the Brāhmin fears/recoils from my voice.'
segments.s236-g492: وزین لشکر is an adjectival phrase 'weighty / dignified army' (وزین = 'dignified, heavy' modifying لشکر = 'army'). گردن‌افراز is a postpositive epithet 'neck‑raising', i.e. 'proud/exalted', and من after it is the possessive 'my' attaching to the whole phrase; classical Persian often stacks epithets this way. The initial و (here fused in وزین) links this noun phrase to the previous clause.
segments.s236-g493: من is the 1st‑person subject 'I'. این زابلی را is the direct object 'this Zābli (man)', with را marking the definite object. بشمشیر is a prepositional/instrumental construction with the prefix بـ 'with' + شمشیر 'sword' and تیز 'sharp' qualifies the sword; the instrument follows the object in this word order.
segments.s236-g494: برآوردگه is a locative/nominal compound 'place of bringing/encounter, place of reckoning'. بر here is a preposition 'upon/against' attached before the verb کنم; کنم is the 1st‑person present/subjunctive of کردن 'I will do / I make', used here with future force. ریز ریز is a reduplicated adverbial 'bit by bit / into small pieces' describing the manner/result of the action. Together the phrase means 'at the place of reckoning I will strike / I will make (him) fall into small pieces.'
segments.s236-g9: پرستندگانی is the plural indefinite/collective 'worshippers/devotees' (‑انی ending). که introduces a relative clause modifying it ('who/that'). نیکو and بدی here are juxtaposed as qualities inside that relative clause: نیکو 'good' followed by بدی 'evil/badness' (both nouns) — together meaning they displayed both good and bad. Word order is noun + relative clause; the verb of the relative clause is omitted (classical Persian often omits auxiliary verbs), so the nouns function as predicates/qualities.
segments.s236-g10: به دیدار و بالا بی‌آهو بدی continues the relative clause and lists spheres/ways in which those qualities appeared. به دیدار = 'in/at (their) meeting/appearance' (the preposition به + noun دیدار). و links two phrases. بالا = 'loftiness/proud bearing'. بی‌آهو is a compound meaning 'without (the) doe' — idiomatically 'without gentleness/grace'; بدی again names 'evil/badness' as the quality manifested. The whole phrase further specifies how the devotees showed good and bad.
segments.s236-g11: از آن ('from that' / 'because of that') sets the cause or source. ساختندی is the past‑plural archaic form of ساختن 'they made/did' (‑ندی = older past plural). بخوان is an infinitival/noun form 'a reading/recital' (here the object made/arranged), and بر خورش = 'upon the stew' (بر + خورش 'stew/dish') marks the target over which the reading was performed. The construction means 'they caused a recitation to be made over the stew.'
segments.s236-g12: بدین گونه is the fixed demonstrative phrase 'in this way' (بدین = بِ + دین/این in older spelling; گونه = manner). بد here qualifies پرورش: 'a bad/ill upbringing' (بد + noun) rather than a separate verb. شاه را uses را as the definite/direct‑object marker on 'the king'. پرورش is a noun 'upbringing/training' — the verb 'they gave' is implied by the previous clause (typical omission in classical Persian), so the sense is 'thus they gave the king a bad upbringing.'
segments.s236-g13: تهمتن is a proper name/epithet. بفرمود is the honorific past 'he commanded/ordered' (ب‑ prefix + فرمود), used here as the main verb. تا before a number marks extent/quantity 'up to/as many as' (so تا سه هزار = 'as many as three thousand'). The phrase is a verbal clause introducing how many were to be made/assembled.
segments.s236-g14: زرهدار برگستوان‌ور سوار is a stacked noun phrase of modifiers + head noun. زرهدار 'gold‑bearing/gilded' and برگستوان‌ور 'strong‑armed/mighty' are adjectival compounds modifying سوار 'horseman/ rider' (the head). Word order is modifier(s) before noun, giving 'gold‑clad, strong‑armed horsemen.'
segments.s236-g15: بدان دژ is the older/preclassical sequence equivalent to به + آن + دژ 'to that fortress' (ب/به + دان/آن fused in older orthography). فرستاد is the simple past 'he sent/ dispatched'. با indicates accompaniment 'with', and گستهم here functions as a companion name or person sent along (in the text it reads as a proper name or epithet). The clause reads 'He sent to that fortress, with Gasteham,...'.
segments.s236-g16: دو گرد خردمند با اوبهم completes the accompaniment: دو گرد 'two گرد' = 'two bodies/companies/divisions' (literally 'two rounds/groups'); خردمند 'wise' modifies گرد. با + اوبهم is an older/conjoined form meaning 'with him/together with him' (او به هم contracted) — it repeats/clarifies accompaniment: 'two companies of wise men with him.' Word order: numeral + noun + adjective + accompaniment phrase.
segments.s236-g233: ز بهر ... خویش = a prepositional phrase meaning 'for the sake of / because of (their) ...'. ز بهر is a classical formula 'for the sake of'; the nouns follow in a coordinated list (بر و بوم و پیوند خویش) with the pronominal suffix -ش marking 'their'. Word order is classical Persian: preposition + purpose + list of nouns + possessive suffix.
segments.s236-g234: زن و کودک خرد و فرزند خویش = a coordinated noun phrase listing the people concerned: 'their wives, small (young) children, and offspring.' خرد here is an adjective after کودک meaning 'small/young.' The final -ش attached to خوی ('kin') marks the possessive 'their.'
segments.s236-g235: همه سر به سر تن به کشتن دهیم = an emphatic subject phrase 'all, one and all' (همه سر به سر) followed by the object and verb. تن به کشتن دهیم literally 'we give the body to killing' — دهیم is the 1st‑person plural present/optative used here to express volition/future ('we shall give' / 'we will offer'). The construction 'به کشتن دادن' indicates causing or yielding to death.
segments.s236-g236: به آید که گیتی به دشمن دهیم = a purposive/result clause introduced by به آید که 'lest / it may happen that'. آید here means 'happen/come to pass.' گیتی به دشمن دهیم = 'we give the world to the enemy,' with به marking direction ('into the hands of the enemy') and دهیم again expressing giving/surrendering.
segments.s236-g237: چو بشنید افراسیاب این سخن = 'When Afrasiab heard these words.' چو = 'when'; بشنید is the past simple 'he heard'; the subject (افراسیاب) follows the verb here, and the object (این سخن) comes at the end, a typical classical Persian word order variation.
segments.s236-g238: فراموش کرد آن نبرد کهن = 'he forgot that ancient battle.' فراموش + کرد is the compound verb 'forgot' (فراموش as root, کرد as auxiliary/past), آن = 'that', and کهن is a postpositive adjective meaning 'ancient/old' modifying نبرد.
segments.s236-g239: بفرمود تا لشکر آراستند = 'He commanded / ordered that the army be arrayed.' بفرمود is an honorific/polite past tense of 'say/command.' تا introduces the subordinate/result clause ('so that / until'), and آراستند is past tense 'they made ready / arrayed themselves.'
segments.s236-g240: بکین نو از جای برخاستند = 'they rose up anew for revenge/from their places.' بکین here has the force related to 'vengeance / taking vengeance' (poetic usage), نو = 'new' (i.e. fresh), از جای = 'from their place(s),' and برخاستند is the past plural 'they rose up / stood up.'
segments.s236-g73: برآورد is a past/perfective third‑person verb formed with the prefix بر‑ + آوردن and here means 'brought forth/produced' (an accomplished action). ازینسان is an adverbial phrase 'thus / in this way' that precedes the verb for emphasis. بافسون is a prepositional phrase 'با + فسون' (archaic spelling of افسون) meaning 'with enchantment' or 'by magic'; و links this clause to the next, and رنج is the noun 'hardship' used here as the object or theme tied to the following clause.
segments.s236-g74: بپالود is a perfective verb (ب‑ prefix + پالودن) meaning 'purified/refined' and takes رنج ('hardship') as its object. The coordinating و links the two verbs; تهی کرد is a compound verbal phrase 'made empty' (تهی + کرد, 'to make empty') where کرد is the light verb 'to do/make' and گنج is the object 'treasure'. The sequence places verbs after their objects (typical classical Persian ordering).
segments.s236-g75: بسی here is an adverb 'much / many' (an intensive adverb placed before the noun). رنج بردند is a past plural verb phrase: رنج (hardship) is the object/theme and بردند (they bore/carried) is the past tense, plural third person — i.e. 'they endured hardships.' مردان مرد is a tautological epithet (plural + singular) meaning 'men of men' = 'valiant men' (مردان = men, مرد here = 'hero').
segments.s236-g76: کزین باره is a prepositional phrase 'from this occasion/undertaking' (کزین = از این, باره = 'occasion/instance'). دژ is the noun 'fortress' and برآرند is a present/subjunctive or jussive plural form (بر‑آرند) meaning 'they raise/bring forth' (here with a purposive sense). گرد at the end functions adverbially 'around/about' (describing the fortress being raised around).
segments.s236-g77: نبدکس is an emphatic negation 'nobody / not a single person'. The sequence بدین here is the fused prepositional phrase 'بدین' (بـ + این) = 'by/with this' or 'in this', used adverbially; in the word list it is split as ب + دین, but in context it functions as the single demonstrative prepositional phrase. شارستان is 'the city/urban settlement' and پادشا is 'king' — the phrase as a whole says 'no one becomes king in/through this city' (classical inverted word order places modifiers before the head).
segments.s236-g78: بدین (again 'by/with this') introduces the means: رنج بردن is a verbal noun + infinitive-like construction where رنج is the noun 'hardship' and بردن (here 'to bear/endure') describes the action. نیارد is an archaic negative/future form (contextually 'does not bring / will not obtain'); the provided word senses do not match that verbal meaning, so its sense index is given as -1. بها is the noun 'value/price/honor' and completes the clause: 'by bearing these hardships one does not gain value/ reward.'
segments.s236-g79: سلیحست is a predicative adjective‑verb 'is armed / is well-equipped' (compound of سَلاح with the copular -ست). The coordinating و links to the locative clause: ایدر 'here' followed by بسی 'much/many' as an intensifier and خوردنی 'food/edibles' as the noun — together 'here is much food' (classical Persian often places the copula before locative phrases).
segments.s236-g80: بزیر اندرون is a locative direction 'down into the interior' (بزیر = downwards/to below, اندرون = inside). راه آوردنی is a verbal/adjectival phrase: راه here as 'way/route' and آوردنی (present‑participle/gerund) 'to be brought' or 'for bringing' — together expressing that the food is brought down/transported into the inner quarters. The participial آوردنی marks a potential or ongoing action.
segments.s236-g377: بیامد is the simple past (3rd singular) 'came'; the verb precedes the title + name here (typical flexible Persian poetic word order). سپهدار is a title 'commander'; پیران here names the individual Piran (proper name). چو begins a simile 'like/as'.
segments.s236-g378: بزرگان و مردان is a coordinated noun phrase 'the great ones and the men/warriors'; و is the conjunction 'and'. روز نبرد is a genitival phrase 'the day of battle' (روز = 'day', نبرد = 'battle'), so the whole clause modifies the simile begun by چو.
segments.s236-g379: ز here is the poetic 'from' ('because of' / 'on the basis of') and governs 'گفتار فرغار' ('Farghār's utterance'). چندی بگفت is a predicate meaning 'he spoke for a while' (چندی = 'for a while', بگفت = past 'he said/spoke' with the b- prefix common in older/poetic Persian). فرغار is a proper name (Farghār).
segments.s236-g380: این که-clause (که ...) introduces the content of his speech: 'تا کیست با او به پیکار جفت'. کی is the interrogative 'who'; ست is the archaic/copular form 'is'. با او = 'with him'; به پیکار جفت means 'paired for combat' or 'his opponent in single combat' (جفت = 'pair' / 'match'). تا here is an emphatic/classical particle and does not carry a clear modern equivalent; it helps form the interrogative phrase 'تا کیست' ('who is it exactly').
segments.s236-g381: بدو is the prepositional phrase 'to him' (ب + او contracting to بدو); گفت here is the past verb 'said' (inverted word order: prepositional object + verb + subject), and پیران is the speaker's name (Piran). که introduces the reported content; ما را uses ما (we/us) with the direct-object/topic marker را, and ز جنگ = 'from war'.
segments.s236-g382: چه چارست is an idiomatic/archaic question 'what good / what is the use' (چارست here is archaic and not one of the simple modern senses listed). جز introduces an exception 'except'; جستن نام و ننگ = 'seeking name (fame) and shame' (جستن infinitive 'to seek', نام 'name/reputation', ننگ 'shame'). The clause expresses Piran's dismissive view of the purpose of war.
segments.s236-g383: چو here means 'when' (introducing a temporal clause). پاسخ چنین یافت is a verbal clause: پاسخ = 'reply/answer', چنین = 'such', یافت is past tense 'he received/found'. افراسیاب is the proper name 'Afrasiab' (subject of the following main clause).
segments.s236-g384: گرفت is the past-of-take/seize 'he took/was seized (with)'; اندران = 'among them/therein' (indicating the target or context); کینه جستن is a noun + infinitive 'the seeking of vengeance' (کینه = 'hatred/grudge', جستن = 'to seek'); شتاب = 'haste' so the whole means he was filled with a hasty desire to exact vengeance.
segments.s236-g25: بفرمود is an honorific/past verb meaning “he commanded/said.” تا introduces a subordinate clause of purpose/result (’that/so that’). تیر باران کنند is an idiomatic verbal phrase: تیر (’arrows’) + باران (figurative ’shower’) with کنند (3rd-person plural subjunctive) to mean ‘they should/they may shower (with) arrows.’ Overall: “he ordered that they rain arrows.”
segments.s236-g26: بریشان is the fused preposition بر + ایشان meaning ‘against/on them.’ کمین is the noun ‘ambush,’ and سواران is the plural ‘horsemen.’ کنند (same verb form as above) is used to indicate the action they are to perform: ‘set/lay an ambush (of) horsemen against them.’ Word order places the prepositional object before the verb, typical of Persian clause structure here.
segments.s236-g27: چنین گفت is a formulaic ‘thus said’ (چنین = ‘thus/so’; گفت = ‘said’). کافور is the speaker’s name. با here means ‘with’ or ‘to’ in the sense ‘together with / addressing’ and سرکشان (plural) refers to those accompanying him (here: scouts/rebellious ones); the phrase as a whole = ‘Thus Kafur said to/with his men.’
segments.s236-g28: که introduces the content (subordinate) clause ‘that…’. سندان is ‘anvil’ (literal metal block). نگیرد is the negative present/subjunctive form of گرفتن (‘not take/receive’), here giving the intended/resulting condition (‘so that the anvil does not take/receive’). ز is the poetic preposition ‘from,’ پیکان is ‘arrow,’ and نشان is ‘mark/trace.’ The clause means roughly ‘that the anvil not receive a mark from the arrow.’
segments.s236-g29: همه is the quantifier ‘all/every,’ placed before the list of arms. تیغ، گرز، و کمند are the listed weapons (sword, mace/club, lasso/snare). آورید is the plural imperative/formal command ‘bring (them).’ The whole segment is an order to fetch these weapons.
segments.s236-g30: سر سرکشان را is the direct object phrase: سر = ‘head,’ سرکشان (plural) = ‘the rebels/scouts/proud ones’ (here the people whose heads are meant), and را is the definite object marker. ببند is an imperative ‘bind/fasten’ (from بستن) and آورید again is the plural imperative ‘bring.’ Together they form two successive commands: ‘bind the heads of the rebels, and bring [them].’
segments.s236-g31: زمانی is a temporal adverb ‘at that time / once / then.’ بران is a classical poetic locution (= بر + ان/آن) meaning ‘upon them/thereon,’ and سان is a comparative particle ‘such/so/like.’ برآویختند is the past tense (3rd-person plural) meaning ‘they sprang up/they leapt upon’ — here describing a sudden attack. Together: ‘at that time they sprang upon them thus.’
segments.s236-g32: که introduces a clause giving the circumstance (‘when/that’). آتش = ‘fire’; ز is the poetic preposition ‘from’; دریا = ‘sea’; برانگیختند is past tense plural ‘they rose up/sprang up.’ The clause literally says ‘that fire rose up from the sea,’ i.e. ‘when fire sprang from the sea.’
segments.s236-g185: مکافات این کار یزدان کند is an optative/subjunctive clause. مکافات (literally “recompense”) is the object/topic, این کار (“this deed”) specifies what will be recompensed, یزدان is the subject placed before the verb (typical Persian word order can be flexible), and کند is third‑person singular present/subjunctive used here with an optative force (“may [God] do/make”).
segments.s236-g186: که introduces a subordinate optative/result clause connected to the previous wish (so that/may). چهر تو is “your face” (noun + enclitic pronoun), همواره is an adverb “always,” خندان is a participial/adjectival form “smiling,” and کند again is third‑person singular present/subjunctive giving the sense “may He make/keep.” Word order is subject (implied God) omitted and predicate final as in Persian.
segments.s236-g187: بپاداش تو نیست‌مان دسترس is an inverted nominal clause: بپاداش “as a reward/for repaying you” + تو “you”; نیست‌مان is نیست with the enclitic ـمـان (our/us) meaning “is not (for us)/we have not,” and دسترس functions as ‘means/ability/accessible reach.’ Together: “We have no means to repay you.”
segments.s236-g188: زبانها پر از آفرینست و بس is a simple predicative phrase. زبانها (pl. “tongues/speeches”) is the subject, پر از = “full of,” آفرینست is a verblike predicate “are praise/are full of praise,” و بس links the clause and بس “enough/sufficient” closes with an emphatic ‘that is enough.’
segments.s236-g189: بزرگیت هر روز بافزون ترست is a comparative clause. بزرگیت (“your greatness”) is the topic, هر روز “every day” gives time, بافزون (here adverbial “increasingly”) + ترست is a classical comparative construction (‑تر + است) meaning “is more/increasing.” Together: “Your greatness becomes greater with each day.”
segments.s236-g190: هنرمند رخش تو صد لشکرست is a verbless/nominal metaphorical clause: هنرمند here describes skill/artistry (an adjectival/nominal sense), رخش تو “your steed” is the possessor phrase, and صد لشکرست is a predicative simile/measure “is a hundred armies.” The final -ست is the copula “is.”
segments.s236-g191: تهمتن بریشان گرفت آفرین is a past narrative clause. تهمتن (proper epithet/name) is the subject; بریشان is a fused prepositional phrase (بر + ایشان) meaning “to/for them”; گرفت is simple past “took/uttered” and here combines with آفرین (“praise”/“bravo”) to mean “he uttered praise for them.”
segments.s236-g192: که آباد بادا بگردان زمین is a following optative content clause introduced by که (here “that/said:”), آباد is the adjective “prosperous,” بادا is an archaic optative particle “may it be,” and بگردان is a causative/imperative‑shaped verb meaning “make/turn (into)” (here used in the sense “may [someone] make”), with زمین “the earth/land” as its object. The sequence expresses a wish: “May the land be made prosperous.”
segments.s236-g201: پس is a temporal connective 'then'. آگاهی ('news/information') is the grammatical subject that آمد ('came/arrived' past) happens to; بافراسیاب is a prepositional/benefactive phrase meaning 'to Afrasiab' (classical word order puts the recipient after the verb). The whole chunk = 'then news came to Afrasiab.'
segments.s236-g202: که is the complementizer 'that' introducing reported content. The phrase بوم و بر is a fixed pair meaning 'land and dwelling/realm'; از دشمنان marks the agent/causer ('by the enemies') in a change-of-state construction, and شد خراب uses شد ('became') plus adjective خراب ('ruined') to mean 'was laid waste'.
segments.s236-g203: دلش is 'his heart' (دل + enclitic ش). زان سخن = 'from that speech/news' (زان = poetic 'from that'), and پر ز تیمار شد literally 'became full of care' (پر 'full (of)' + ز تیمار 'of care' + شد 'became'). Word order places the resultant predicate before the verb 'شد'.
segments.s236-g204: همه پرنیان = 'all the silken robes' (subject), بر تنش = 'upon his body' (prepositional phrase with enclitic ش), and خار شد = 'became thorns' (شد as change-of-state verb + خار 'thorn'); the clause means comforts (silks) turned into pain (thorns).
segments.s236-g205: بدل گفت = 'in return/reply he said' (بدل adverbially 'in return', گفت here = 'said' in context). پیگار او کار کیست is an interrogative: پیگار او 'his fighting/struggle' + کار کیست 'what is the matter/what sort of thing is it?' with کیست = 'what is / who is' (interrogative + copula).
segments.s236-g206: سپاهست is the fused form of سپاه است 'it is an army/there is an army'; بسیار qualifies it as 'large/many'. The conjunction و links to سالار کیست 'and who is the commander?' — کیست is the interrogative 'who is' with the copula unstressed.
segments.s236-g207: گر is the conditional 'if'. آنست رستم = 'that is Rostam' (آن + است) and the relative clause که من دیده‌ام 'whom I have seen' (که introducing the clause, دیده‌ام = present perfect 'I have seen').
segments.s236-g208: بسی از نبردش بپیچیده‌ام: بسی (classical 'much/often') modifies the clause; از marks source/agent ('from his battle' = 'in the face of his fighting'); بپیچیده‌ام is a perfect form of پیچیدن with the verbal prefix ب‑ here meaning 'I have turned aside / been turned aside' (poetic sense 'to be routed / to give way'). The whole means 'I have often turned aside/been put to flight by his battles.'
segments.s236-g121: This phrase shows a poetic/inverted word order: the locative prepositional phrase در دژ (in the citadel) comes first, followed by the past verb ببست (he/they shut). آن زمان is a temporal adverbial 'at that time' and جنگجوی is the agent 'the warrior(s)'; in Ferdowsi's style the subject can appear after the verb. ببست is a past (preterite) form meaning 'shut/closed.'
segments.s236-g122: بتاراج and کشتن are the actions that follow; they function as objects/activities governed by the verb نهادند. نهادند is the past plural 'they set/put' used in the light-verb construction 'نهادن روی' meaning 'to set about' or 'to direct oneself to.' روی here is the noun 'face' used idiomatically (روی نهادن) = 'to set upon/turn to.'
segments.s236-g123: چه مایه is an exclamatory measure phrase 'what an amount' / 'how many'; it intensifies the following verb. بکشتند is the past plural 'they killed.' و connects to the next phrase; چندی (here) means 'many/some' modifying اسیر 'captives.'
segments.s236-g124: ببردند is the past plural 'they took/carried off.' زان is a contracted prepositional element 'from that' (زان شهر = from that city). برنا و پیر ('young and old') are coordinated adjectives describing the captives; the list-style order is typical of Persian enumerations.
segments.s236-g125: بسی here is an intensive quantifier ('much/many'); it precedes a list of valuables: سیم و زر (silver and gold) and گرانمایه چیز ('precious things'). Persian often strings wealth-items without an explicit verb to list spoils.
segments.s236-g126: This continues the inventory: ستور ('beasts/cattle'), غلام ('slaves'), پرستار ('attendants/caretakers') with نیز ('also') marking that these were included as well. The nouns are coordinated and presented as items of plunder; there is no overt verb in this nominal list because it ties to the prior verbal context.
segments.s236-g127: تهمتن is the proper name (Tahmatan). بیامد is the narrative past 'came/arrived.' سر و تن ('head and body') is a common Hebraic-style pairing in Persian; بشست is the past 'he washed' and its subject is the preceding proper name (Tahmatan), so the clause means 'Tahmatan came and washed his head and body.'
segments.s236-g128: بپیش here means 'into the presence of / before.' جهانداور is a title/name 'the world-judge' or 'ruler'; آمد is 'came.' نخست is an adverb 'first' placed after the verb, so the clause reads 'he came first before the World-judge.'
segments.s236-g281: چو begins a simile meaning "like/as" and introduces a coordinated list of names/titles. The nouns کاموس و منشور are simply joined by و, and خاقان چین reads as a title plus place ("Khaghan of China") where the place follows the title in the classical style instead of using a genitive marker.
segments.s236-g282: This clause continues the comparison. گهار is another proper/name-like item in the listing; چو again marks a simile for the following descriptive noun گرگوی ("wolf‑like"), and با + آفرین is an adverbial phrase meaning "with acclaim/praise."
segments.s236-g283: دگر functions as an additive particle "another/besides." کندر و شنگل are a coordinated pair (either items or names). آن شاه هند is a noun phrase "that king of Hind/India" placed after the list as a specification or appositive; classical Persian freely orders such apposition.
segments.s236-g284: سپاهی is a noun meaning "army/host" (subject or topical noun). ز is the poetic preposition "from." تا here means "as far as" (extent marker) and پیش has the sense "in the presence of/ before/at the frontier of," so تا پیش سند gives the extent "from Kashmir up to Sind."
segments.s236-g285: بنیروی is a prepositional/agentive phrase "by the power/virtue of." این رستم is the demonstrative + name "this Rostam." شیر گیر is an epithet in apposition: شیر ("lion") + گیر (agentive "catcher/seizer") gives "lion-catcher," placed after the name as a descriptive title.
segments.s236-g286: بکشتند and بردند are simple past tense, 3rd‑person plural verbs "they killed" and "they carried/took (away)." چندی modifies اسیر as an indefinite quantifier "some/several," so چندی اسیر is the object "several captives." The coordinating و links the two past verbs.
segments.s236-g287: چهل روز is a time expression "forty days." بالشکر is a prepositional/adverbial compound (با + شکر) meaning "with thanks/with thanksgiving" in classical usage. آویز is an adjective/participial form "hanging" or "suspended," and بود is the past copula "was," so the clause states a state continuing for forty days.
segments.s236-g288: گهی ... گه ... is a paired adverbial construction meaning "at times ... and at times ..." Here گهی رزم = "sometimes (there was) battle," گه بزم = "sometimes (there was) feasting/banquet," and پرهیز بود adds "and there was restraint/abstinence." The final بود is again the past copula describing states or alternations.
segments.s236-g105: فرود آمد is a past-tense verbal phrase meaning 'descended/alighted' and appears before its subject (poetic inversion). آن باره is a temporal adverbial phrase 'at that occasion/then' following the verb. تور is the proper-name subject placed after the verb (typical of Ferdowsi's poetic word order). گرد here is an adverb/prepositional-like word 'around/about' modifying the coming action that follows.
segments.s236-g106: ز هر سو is a prepositional phrase 'from every side' (ز = from, هر سو = each/ every side). سپاه اندر آمد is literally 'a host/army came in' with اندر (archaic/prepositional 'in/among') + آمد (past 'came'). The final بگرد is an imperative/verb-form of گشتن ('turn/go about') used here as an emphatic adverbial/phrasing meaning 'around' or 'on every side', attached to the coming action.
segments.s236-g107: بفرمود here is the honorific/past reporting verb 'he commanded/said' with the subject رستم (Rostam) following it (poetic order). که introduces the content clause (that/that you...), and جنگ آورید is a jussive/imperative sense: آوردید (2nd plural imperative/subjunctive form) with جنگ as its object/goal 'wage/make war' (i.e., 'set upon battle').
segments.s236-g108: This is a direct command with the objects fronted: کمانها 'the bows' (plural) and تیر خدنگ 'barbed/pointed arrows' (خدنگ qualifies تیر). آورید is the same imperative/command form 'bring' (2nd plural), governing the listed war-equipment.
segments.s236-g109: گوان is an archaic/distributive marker meaning 'some/one set' (used here to introduce one group). از پی is a prepositional idiom 'in pursuit of/after', followed by the coordinated objects گنج و فرزند خویش 'treasure and their offspring'. Note خویش is split orthographically into خوی + ش (the possessive clitic ش = 'his/their'), so 'فرزند خویش' = 'their child/offspring.'
segments.s236-g110: همان functions contrastively ('the other/the same' meaning 'others' here). Again از پی 'in pursuit of' governs بوم و پیوند خویش 'homeland and kinship/relations' (پیوند = kin/bond). The clause parallels the previous one, with خویش+ش marking the possessive 'their.'
segments.s236-g111: همه is the subject 'all/everyone.' سر بدادند literally 'they gave their heads' (بدادند = past 'they gave'), an idiom meaning they were killed/sacrificed or lost their heads. یکسر is an adverb 'entirely/altogether' and بباد (بِ باد) is a poetic expression 'to the wind' meaning 'lost/ swept away.' The sequence is idiomatic and emphatic.
segments.s236-g112: This short clause uses the comparative adjective گرامی‌تر 'more honored/more dear.' آنکو is a relative pronoun 'he who/that which.' ز مادر 'from/of the mother' and نزاد is an archaic form related to زاد/زادَن meaning 'birth' or 'was born' (here expressing 'who was born of a mother'). The whole line praises as more honorable the one born of a mother (note the archaic/poetic ordering).
segments.s236-g353: شب تیره is a noun phrase 'dark night' (modifier تیره after the noun). بگشاد is a past verb 'opened' (ب‑ prefix on an older past form) and چشم دژم is the object/phrase 'its sullen eye' with دژم as postpositive adjective. Word order is Subject (شب تیره) – Verb (بگشاد) – Object (چشم دژم).
segments.s236-g354: ز here means 'because of / from' (causal use). پشت ماه 'the back/side of the moon' is the affected noun. اندر آمد is a compound where اندر = 'into / in' and آمد = past 'came'; بخم is a predicative noun/adverb meaning 'obscurity/gloom' (archaic). The whole phrase says that, because of sorrow, the moon's back 'came into' gloom.
segments.s236-g355: جهان is the subject 'the world'. گشت is a past copular/transformative verb 'became/turned'. برسان here functions idiomatically with the following noun to indicate being suffused or transformed 'into/like' (literally 'bring to'), and مشک سیاه is the complement 'black musk' (a metaphor for darkness/gloom).
segments.s236-g356: چو = 'when'. فرغار is a proper name (or epithet) here (the commander/leader). برگشت is the past verb 'returned'. ز ایران is a prepositional phrase 'from Iran' (ز = from) and سپاه 'host/army' is the noun (poetic word order places سپاه after the verb).
segments.s236-g357: بیامد is a past simple 'came' (here narrative past). بنزدیک is an adverb/prepositional adverb meaning 'near/close at hand' and it precedes the proper name افراسیاب, the addressee/target of the approach (poetic order: verb + adverb + name).
segments.s236-g358: This is a time phrase: شب تیره = 'the dark night', هنگام = 'at the time/at the hour of', and آرام و خواب = 'rest and sleep' (آرام used as 'rest/quiet' here). Together they specify when Afrāsīāb approached: in the dark hour of repose.
segments.s236-g359: چنین 'thus/so' introduces reported speech. گفت here functions as the reporting verb 'said' (the provided lemma senses list is dialogic, but the usage is the verb 'he said'). کز is the fused relative/causal 'that/from which' linking to the following phrase, and بارگاه بلند = 'the lofty/lofty-sounding court/palace' (کز بارگاه بلند = 'from the lofty court').
segments.s236-g360: برفتم is an archaic past 1st‑person form 'I went / I departed' (ب‑ prefix on رفتن in older/poetic style) used as the main verb of the reported speech. سوی = 'toward' (preposition), followed by the proper name رستم and the epithet/place دیوبند in apposition (Rostam of Dīvband). Note: the lemma senses supplied for برفتم do not match this verbal usage, so the sense index is set to -1.
segments.s236-g49: This is a compact, somewhat inverted clause: یکی is an adverbial/indefinite particle ('one/once' — here 'all at once' or 'a certain time'), حمله is the noun 'attack', آورد is the past tense verb 'brought/made' (here 'made/launched'), کافور is the subject placed after the verb (poetic inversion) and سخت is a postpositive adjective/adverb modifying the attack ('severe/fierce').
segments.s236-g50: بران is a locative/adverbial particle 'upon that/thereon' linking this phrase to what was struck; بارور and خسروانی are adjectival modifiers ('fruitful/fruit‑bearing' and 'royal/kingly') that together qualify درخت ('tree'). Word order is poetic (adjectives precede the noun here).
segments.s236-g51: بینداخت is the simple past 'threw/cast'. تیغی is used adjectivally/nominally as 'blade/sword‑like' (a blade) and بکردار is a poetic formation meaning 'in the form/shape of' (lit. 'with the aspect of'), together qualifying تیر 'arrow' — so the phrase describes an 'arrow shaped like a blade' that was cast.
segments.s236-g52: که introduces a relative or purpose clause ('which/that'); آید is present/subjunctive 'may come' or 'might strike' (possibility); مگر here is a small poetic particle indicating contingency/expectation (introducing the intended target); بر is the preposition 'upon/against'; یل is 'hero/brave man' and شیرگیر is an epithet 'lion‑catcher' modifying یل. Together: 'which might come upon the lion‑catching hero.'
segments.s236-g53: بپیش اندر is a compound adverbial ('forward/toward the front'); آورد is the past verb 'brought/put'; the proper name رستم follows the verb (poetic inversion) as the agent; سپر is the direct object 'shield'. Literal order is verb + subject + object but the meaning is 'Rostam brought/put his shield forward.'
segments.s236-g54: فرو ماند is a compound predication meaning 'fell down / remained down / was driven down' (فرو = down, ماند = remained/was left). کافور is the subject (placed after the verb), and پرخاشخر is an epithet 'quarrelsome/aggressive one' qualifying him. Together: 'Kafur the quarrelsome was left/fell down.'
segments.s236-g55: کمندی is the object 'a lasso/lariat', and بینداخت is the past 'threw/cast'. بر سوی + طوس is the prepositional phrase 'toward Tus' (سوی 'toward' with the proper name طوس). The verb precedes the directional phrase for emphasis: 'he threw a lasso toward Tus.'
segments.s236-g56: بسی کرد is an idiomatic verb phrase 'did much / exerted himself greatly' (بسی 'much' + کرد 'did'); رستم is the agent (post‑verbal for emphasis). برو can be read as 'he went' or an imperative 'go' depending on context; here in narrative it functions as a verb 'went on' or 'pressed on' and بر فسوس ('against the فسوس') names the target — فسوس meaning an insidious whisper/evil suggestion. So: 'Rostam did much and went against the insidious whisper.'
segments.s236-g409: This is a temporal/adverbial clause headed by چو (‘when/like’). سپهکش is an epithet ‘army‑chief’ or ‘military champion’ placed before چو; چو here introduces a temporal/ comparative clause meaning ‘when’ or ‘as’. رستم سپهدار طوس is an appositive/name phrase (proper name + title + name) that follows the particle to identify the army‑chief.
segments.s236-g410: بابر is the agent (proper name) performing the action; اندر is a locative preposition ‘among/among them’; اورده is a past participial/compound verb meaning ‘has brought’ or ‘has caused to come’ (here: ‘has raised/sounded’); آوای کوس is the direct object ‘the sound/cry of the war‑drum’. Word order is Agent + locative + verb + object, typical in poetic diction.
segments.s236-g411: چو here functions as a conditional ‘if’. بدست تو is a prepositional/locative phrase ‘into your hand(s)’ (بدست = ‘into the hand’); گردد (from گشت/گرد) is a present/subjunctive/optative form ‘becomes/comes into’ used in the protasis; تباه is a post‑verbal predicate adjective meaning ‘ruined’ or ‘destroyed’ describing the subject.
segments.s236-g412: نیابد is a classical negative‑form verb meaning ‘will not find’ (negated present/future sense). سپهر is the logical subject ‘the sky/heavens’ placed after the verb in inverted poetic order. اندرین مرز راه is a locational/phrase ‘a way/path in these borders’ (اندرین = ‘in these’, مرز = ‘frontiers/limits’, راه = ‘path’); together the clause says the heavens find no way through these borders.
segments.s236-g413: همه مرز را is a fronted object/topic phrase ‘all (the) border’ (را marks definiteness/emphasis). زویست is an archaic fused construction meaning ‘is from him / belongs to him’ (ز = ‘from’ + او + است). و بس is a short emphatic addition ‘and enough / and that is sufficient’. Word order emphasizes the object before the copular phrase.
segments.s236-g414: تو باش is an imperative address ‘you be…’ (باش = imperative ‘be’). اندرین کار is a locative/expressive phrase ‘in this affair/matter’. فریادرس is a predicate noun ‘rescuer / helper’; together the sentence commands the addressee to act as rescuer in this business.
segments.s236-g415: گر introduces the conditional ‘if’. او را marks the third‑person object with the definite object marker را. بدست تو آید is the phrasal idea ‘comes into your hand(s)’ (بدست = ‘into the hand’, آید = ‘comes’); زمان here functions as a temporal adverb/noun ‘then / the time’ tying this protasis to the apodosis that follows.
segments.s236-g416: شود is the future/optative auxiliary ‘will become’. رام is the predicate adjective ‘tame / subdued’. روی زمین is an adverbial locution ‘upon the earth / in the world’ (روی = ‘upon/on’, زمین = ‘earth’). بی‌گمان is a final adverb ‘undoubtedly / surely’. The clause therefore asserts a sure future result: he will be subdued on earth.
segments.s236-g321: This is a verbless (or existential) clause typical of classical Persian: the logical subject 'مرا' (me) appears before the oblique marker 'را', then the comitative phrase 'با دلاور' ('with the brave one'), followed by the quantity 'بسی' and the copula 'بود' plus the noun 'جنگ'. Word order is Topic/Object – with/partner – quantifier – copula – head noun; together it yields 'I had many wars/fights with the brave one.'
segments.s236-g322: The phrase 'یکی جوشنستش ز چرم پلنگ' describes one specific piece of his armor. 'یکی' is an indefinite/numbering word ('one' / 'a certain'), 'جوشنستش' (jowshan + -eš) means 'his cuirass/armor', 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from', and 'چرم پلنگ' identifies the material as 'leopard hide'. The sequence is determiner – possessed noun – preposition – material.
segments.s236-g323: 'سلیحم نیامد برو کارگر' expresses that the speaker's weapon(s) were ineffective. 'سلیحم' is 'my weapon(s)', 'نیامد' here means 'did not avail / was not of use', 'برو' in this archaic/poetic positioning modifies the following adjective or verb and is not the ordinary imperative 'go' (its usual senses do not fit, so the precise function is idiomatic), and 'کارگر' means 'effective/useful'. The whole yields 'my weapon did not prove effective.'
segments.s236-g324: 'بسی آزمودم بگرز و تبر' is a past-tense personal clause: 'بسی' quantifies ('much' / 'many'), 'آزمودم' is the 1st-person past 'I tried/tested', and the following words enumerate the arms tried. 'بگرز' here names a weapon (a mace) but the listed lemma/senses do not match that meaning, so its precise lexical sense is idiomatic/archiac; 'و تبر' is 'and the axe'.
segments.s236-g325: 'کنون آزمون را یکی کارزار' is an object phrase introduced by time adverb 'کنون' ('now'). 'آزمون را' marks 'the trial' as definite object, 'یکی کارزار' qualifies it as 'one battle' or 'a single combat' — together: 'now (let us) make a trial, one battle.' The order is temporal – object – numeral + noun.
segments.s236-g326: 'بسازیم تا چون بود روزگار' gives purpose/result: 'بسازیم' is 1st-person plural present/subjunctive 'let us make/let us set up', 'تا' introduces the purpose 'so that', 'چون' = 'how', and 'بود روزگار' = 'the state of the times/beings of the age'. The clause reads 'let us set up (this trial) so that we may see how the times are.'
segments.s236-g327: 'گر ایدونک یزدان بود یارمند' opens a conditional: 'گر' = 'if', 'ایدونک' is a small, dialectal/poetic particle (here left untranslated as an epistemic/hedging particle like 'perhaps' or 'by chance'), 'یزدان' = 'God', 'بود' = 'were/was', and 'یارمند' = 'a helper/ally'. The clause means 'If indeed God were/turned out to be a helper.'
segments.s236-g328: 'بگردد ببایست چرخ بلند' gives the consequent/result: 'بگردد' (subjunctive/impersonal) = 'would turn / would be turned', 'ببایست' = 'must / would have to', and 'چرخ بلند' = 'the high/lofty wheel' (a common image for fate/heaven). Together: 'the high wheel (of fate) would have to turn.'
segments.s236-g257: ز بیگانگان جای پردخته کرد is a compact past‑tense clause: ز (poetic از) means 'from/among', بیگانگان is the plural 'foreigners/strangers', جای پردخته کرد literally 'made/appointed a place' (پردخته = arranged/prepared; کرد = did/made). Word order is typical Persian: prepositional phrase (ز بیگانگان) + object (جای پردخته) + verb (کرد).
segments.s236-g258: بفرغار گفت ای گرانمایه مرد: بفرغار here is the addressee/name (to Farghār), گفت is the simple past 'said', and ای گرانمایه مرد is a vocative phrase — 'O esteemed/precious man'. The sequence is [to‑Farghār] گفت [vocative].
segments.s236-g259: هم‌اکنون برو سوی ایران سپاه is an imperative instruction: هم‌اکنون 'right now', برو 'go' (imperative), سوی ایران سپاه 'toward the Iranian army' (سوی = toward, ایران = Iran, سپاه = the army/host). Word order: temporal adverb + imperative + directional phrase + target noun.
segments.s236-g260: نگه کن بدین رستم رزمخواه: نگه کن is an imperative 'keep watch / hold / stand ready' (نگه = 'watch', کن = 'do' imperative). بدین here introduces the recipient/target ('for this' / 'to this'), followed by the name رستم and the epithet رزمخواه 'war‑seeking/aspiring fighter'. The whole means 'keep watch for this Rostam the warrior.' Note: بدین is tokenized here as ب + دین; the intended unit is بدین = 'for this'.
segments.s236-g261: سواران نگه کن که چنداند و چون is an instruction to the cavalry: سواران 'horsemen' (vocative/subject), نگه کن 'keep watch' (imperative), که introduces the subordinate purpose/result 'so that/that', چنداند 'how many they are', و چون 'and how / in what manner'. The sequence expresses purpose: keep watch so that you may learn number and manner.
segments.s236-g262: که دارد برین بوم و بر رهنمون continues the subordinate clause: که 'who/that', دارد (here simple present/persistent form) 'are/are positioned' or 'have', بر + این بوم = 'on this land' (ین is the orthographic variant of این = 'this'), و بر رهنمون 'and on the route/path'. The verb دارد links the relative clause to the preceding چنداند/چون.
segments.s236-g263: وزان نامداران پرخاشجوی: وزان = 'and from that/of those', linking to what was just mentioned; نامداران 'famous/renowned men', پرخاشجوی 'quarrelsome / aggressive'. This is a noun phrase identifying a class of men from whom information will be sought.
segments.s236-g264: ببینی که چنداند و بر چند روی is a continuation: ببینی (subjunctive/optative of دیدن) 'you should/will see', که 'that', چنداند 'how many they are', و بر چند روی 'and on how many faces/sides' — روی here means 'face/side' (i.e., fronts). The clause asks the watcher to observe both number and the multiplicity of fronts/sides they occupy.
segments.s236-g385: بپیران is a prepositional/indirect-object phrase 'to the elders' (ب‑ = to). بفرمود is an honorific past-tense verb 'he commanded/said'. تا here introduces a purpose clause ('so that / in order that'). با سپاه is a simple prepositional phrase 'with an army' placed after the verb phrase; note Persian uses با before the noun.
segments.s236-g386: بیاید is a subjunctive/jussive 3rd-person form 'may/should come' used after تا to mark purpose. بر is the preposition marking the target/direction 'against' (here: against Rostam). رستم is the proper name. کینه‌خواه is an epithet in apposition 'the Avenger' placed after the name.
segments.s236-g387: ز پیش + noun means 'from before / out of the front (of)'; سپهبد is the commander (spahbed). به بیرون کشید is a verb phrase 'pulled/led out (into the open)', with به showing direction 'to/into' and بیرون 'out'. کشید is the past of کشیدن 'to pull/lead'.
segments.s236-g388: همی is an archaic/poetic iterative/continuous particle meaning roughly 'then/continually'. رزم را uses را as the definite object marker ('the battle' as object). سوی هامون is a directional phrase 'toward the Hamun (plain)'. کشید again is the past of 'to draw/lead', placed at the end as the main verb.
segments.s236-g389: خروش آمد is a subject-verb pair 'a roar came/rose' (آمد past). از دشت is a prepositional phrase 'from the plain'. و joins the next nominal phrase: آوای کوس means 'the sound/voice of the war-drum' (possessive/genitive meaning 'sound of the drum').
segments.s236-g390: جهان شد uses شد as an intransitive copular/ result verb 'the world became' or 'the world was [so]'. ز introduces the cause or source ('because of / from'). گرد here functions as 'dust' (the dust kicked up by an army). سپاه آبنوس is a noun + postnominal adjective 'the ebony army' or 'black (clad) host' (Persian places the modifying adjective after the noun).
segments.s236-g391: سپه بود is a simple nominal clause 'the host/army was'. چندانک is an adverbial 'to such an extent / so much'. گفتی here is a poetic modal/parenthetical 'one would say / you might say' (comparable to گویی); the phrase چندانک گفتی qualifies the following statement about جهان.
segments.s236-g392: همی گردَد combines the iterative/emphatic همی with گردد (a form of گشتن/گردیدن 'become/turn') to mean 'it seems / it becomes (so)'. از marks cause ('by/from the dust of ...'). گرد again means 'dust' here; اسپان (pl. of اسب) = 'horses' (i.e. the cavalry), and نهان is an adjective 'hidden', placed after the noun phrase.
segments.s236-g193: This short clause states possession/support using an older word order: 'مرا' (to me / for me) + 'پشت' (back/support) + 'ز آزادگان' (of the free) + the copular ending '-ست' attached to 'آزادگان' (is of the free) and the adjective 'راست' (true/upright). In classical Persian the possessor relation can appear with an object-like 'مرا' up front; the sense is “I have (for me) the backing of the nobles, which is true.”
segments.s236-g194: 'دل روشنم' is a predicative phrase where the adjective 'روشن' takes the first‑person enclitic '-م' to mean 'my heart is clear/bright.' 'بر زبانم' literally 'on my tongue' functions as a locative/instrumental phrase meaning 'my tongue (is) witness,' and 'گواست' is a copular/predicative verb 'is witness' (poetic/archaic). The two halves are coordinated: statement of inward purity followed by the tongue testifying to it.
segments.s236-g195: 'ازان پس' is a temporal/sequence marker 'after that/thereupon,' followed by 'چنین گفت' ('thus said' — literal formulaic narration). 'کایدر سه روز' is an archaic/dense sequence where 'کایدر' corresponds to classical 'که در' ('that in/for') introducing a duration 'سه روز' ('three days'): together it yields 'that for three days...'.
segments.s236-g196: The verb form 'بباشیم' is a subjunctive/jussive first‑person plural used as a hortative/imperative 'let us be / let us remain.' It governs the adjectives 'شادان' ('joyful') and the coordinated phrase 'و گیتی فروز' where 'گیتی فروز' is a compound adjective 'world‑bright' (make the world radiant). The clause is an exhortation: 'let us be joyful and brighten the world.'
segments.s236-g197: This is a temporal/directional prepositional phrase: 'چهارم' (the fourth [day]) is understood adverbially, followed by 'سوی جنگ افراسیاب' where 'سوی' (toward) precedes the object 'جنگ افراسیاب' ('Afrasiab's battle'). Classical Persian frequently places the direction marker before the noun it governs.
segments.s236-g198: 'برانیم' is a first‑person plural verbal form (hortative/subjunctive) meaning roughly 'let us drive/advance.' It is followed by the coordinating 'و' and the clause 'آتش برآریم ز آب' where 'برآریم' (let us raise/bring) is another hortative; 'ز آب' ('from the water') is a poetic ablative. Together the verbs form coordinated commands: 'let us drive (them) and let us bring/raise fire from the water.'
segments.s236-g199: 'همه نامداران' (all the renowned ones) is the subject noun phrase. 'بگفتار اوی' uses the prepositional/administrative prefix 'ب-' + 'گفتار' (word/command) and the possessive/pronominal 'اوی' ('his') to mean 'at his command' or 'according to his word.' The phrase sets the condition under which the subject acts.
segments.s236-g200: This sequence lists the nobles' actions. The first item 'ببزم' is a problematic/formulaic form in the manuscript (not a common modern verb) but functions here as 'they went to the feast' (contextualized by 'بخوردند' next). 'بخوردند' is a past plural 'they ate' or 'they partook,' and 'نهادند' is the past plural of 'نهادن' ('they placed/they set'); when followed by 'روی' ('face') the composite 'نهادند روی' idiomatically means 'they turned/set their faces (toward a direction)' — i.e. they prepared to move. The clause is thus a sequence: feasted, ate, then turned to depart.
segments.s236-g457: A prepositional/relative noun phrase: ز + خون سیاوش = 'from the blood of Siavash'. The relative clause introduced by که (here 'that/which') modifies خون: 'which was on his hand.' اوی is an older enclitic form of 'his/her' (او + ی) attached to دست, so بر دست اوی = 'on his hand.' Word order places the relative clause after the head noun, as in classical Persian.
segments.s236-g458: An interrogative/result clause introduced by چه آمد 'what came/what resulted'. ز here is the preposition 'from' governing the two nouns that follow: پرخاش 'reproach' and (poetically) گفت و گوی 'words and talk'. وز appears as a fused connective (functioning like 'and/from') and is not used with the literal senses supplied in the list, so treat it as a poetic connective here.
segments.s236-g459: A prepositional/listing phrase introduced by ز 'about' or 'concerning' followed by a coordinated list: خاقان و منشور و کاموس. The final word گرد here functions adverbially/postpositionally to mark 'about/concerning' (classical usage: [list] گرد = 'concerning the ...').
segments.s236-g460: A short clause: گذشته سخنها = 'past speeches/tales' (گذشته modifies سخنها), همه = 'all', and برشمرد is the past tense verb 'he enumerated/recounted.' Classical word order places modifiers before the noun and the quantifier (همه) before the verb.
segments.s236-g461: Reported-speech frame: بگفت 'he said' + content introducer آنک (classical 'that'). این رنجم 'this trouble/suffering' is the subject of the following nominal predicate از یک تنست 'is from one person/body' (تنست = تن + -ست, 'is a body/person'). The verb is the copula -ست in an archaic fused form.
segments.s236-g462: A relative/explanatory clause introduced by که 'that/who': او را پلنگینه پیراهنست. In classical Persian construction, 'او را ... هست/است' can express possession or wearing ('he has' / 'he is clothed with'); here it means 'he has/wears a leopard‑skin tunic.' را functions with او in a dative/possessive-like role, and پیراهنست is the archaic fused form of 'پیراهن است' (is a shirt/tunic).
segments.s236-g463: A negative-result clause: نیامد 'did not come' here functions idiomatically with a predicate to mean 'did not prove/avail.' سلیحم 'my weapon' (possessive -م) is the subject, بدو 'to/against him' indicates the target, and کارگر is an adjectival predicate 'effective' — together: 'my weapon did not prove effective against him.'
segments.s236-g464: A sequence introduced by an adverbial/transition بران ('thereupon' / 'upon that') followed by an imperative or command. ببر here is best read as the imperative 'take!' (classical/poetic) and is followed by a coordinated list of objects: و آن خود 'and that cuirass/that very (خود used as the named object here)' and چینی سپر 'and the Chinese shield.' Word order is imperative + direct objects.
segments.s236-g169: نشاید is an impersonal negative verb meaning 'it is not fitting' (3rd-person impersonal). که introduces the subordinate clause giving what is not fitting. بی‌آفرین qualifies تو ('you' addressed here, vocative/poetic), and لب is the noun 'lip' that will combine with the following verb to form the idiom لب گشودن 'to open one's lips / to speak to'. Word order places the object/phrase 'لب' before the verb in the next segment.
segments.s236-g170: گشاییم is 1st-person plural subjunctive/optative ('that we open' / 'we should open') used inside the clause introduced by که. زین پس is a frozen adverbial phrase 'henceforth / from now on' and بروز و بشب is an adverbial pair 'by day and by night' (bروز = by day, ب شب = by night) modifying the verb. The verb completes the idiom لب گشودن begun in the previous segment.
segments.s236-g171: کسی کو here functions as a relative phrase 'whoever/anyone who' (classical usage where کو ~ 'who'). بپیمود is past tense 'traversed' (3rd person) and روی زمین 'on the surface of the earth' is the locative phrase; the relative clause describes an indefinite person who has travelled the earth.
segments.s236-g172: This is the main clause: جهان دید 'saw the world' with دید in past tense. The following nouns joined by و ('and') — آرام 'calm', پرخاش 'assault/aggression', کین 'hatred' — enumerate aspects of the world that such a traveller has witnessed; the coordination places contrasting qualities side by side.
segments.s236-g173: This clause expresses a universal negative: بیک جای (literally 'in any place') + ... ندید 'did not see' means 'nowhere was an army greater than this seen'. بیش here is comparative 'more/greater (than this)', and لشکر is the noun 'army'. The past negative ندید is used to state that such a sight did not occur anywhere.
segments.s236-g174: نه coordinates with the previous negative to form 'nor' (neither ... nor). از موبد سالخورده شنید is 'heard from an aged mobed' with شنید in past tense; here the sense is 'to hear of / to learn about' (i.e. even an old priest had not heard of such an army). 'از' marks the agent/source of the hearing.
segments.s236-g175: This is the start of a list introduced by the preposition ز ('from/of'). شاهان و پیلان lists 'kings and elephants.' وز here is actually a fused 'و ز' ('and from') linking to the next item; تخت عاج is a compound 'ivory throne' (تخت 'throne' + عاج 'ivory'). The structure is paratactic: repeated prepositions introduce enumerated items.
segments.s236-g176: Continuation of the same enumerative pattern: ز مردان و اسپان 'from men and horses' then و از گنج و تاج 'and from treasure and crowns'.
