The repeated prepositions and conjunctions list the various categories (people, animals, objects, wealth) associated with the army or its spoils.
segments.s236-g473: بدرید is a past perfective verb form (with poetic/preverbal ب-) meaning 'tore' and the verb comes first in this short clause. پهلوی (object) is followed by the possessor phrase دیو سپید (the demon, qualified by 'white'), so the phrase reads literally 'tore (the) flank of the white demon' — the object marker is omitted and the agent is understood from context.
segments.s236-g474: This is a nominal phrase with an asyndetic list: جگرگاه (the epigastrium / vital region) followed by three nouns in apposition (پولاد, غندی, و بید). There is no copula; Persian poetry often omits 'is' so the sequence reads as a descriptive list — 'his jigar‑gah: steel, a grain, and a willow.'
segments.s236-g475: مرا (here split in the token list as م + را) is a fronted object/dative used for emphasis ('for me'); نیست is the existential/negative copula 'is not'. پایاب is the predicate noun ('steadiness/lasting state' in older/poetic usage) and با جنگ اوی is a prepositional phrase 'with/against his fighting' that attaches to the predicate. Word order places the emphasized personal object before the verb, a common poetic pattern.
segments.s236-g476: نیارم functions here as a negative stative/auxiliary cluster ('I lack / I will not have [a helper/refuge]') — in classical usage یار can mean 'helper' so نیارم = 'I have no helper' (hence, 'I will not be without...'). ببد is a poetic/perfective form related to 'become/turn' and together with the verbal noun کردن forms a periphrasis meaning 'to make/turn (one's) intention/aim'. آهنگ اوی is the object 'his intent/aim'. The clause therefore expresses resolve: 'I will not be without [help]; I will turn my intent toward him.'
segments.s236-g477: This is an optative/blessing clause: تن و جان من ('my body and soul') is the theme, پیش رای تو ('before/at your counsel') is a locative/prepositional phrase placing them 'at your counsel', and باد is the optative particle 'may' (a fixed classical Persian wish form). The possessor تو follows رای (râye to = 'your counsel').
segments.s236-g478: همیشه (adverb) 'always' modifies the following nominal phrase خرد رهنمای تو ('wisdom [to be] your guide'). Again باد is the optative particle 'may,' so the whole clause reads 'may wisdom always be your guide.' Word order places the adverb first for emphasis, then the noun phrase, with تو marking possession.
segments.s236-g479: من (subject) + او را (direct object with را for emphasis) places 'him' as the focus. بر اندیشه داشتن is an idiom meaning 'to have (someone) in thought / to intend (to do something to them)'; دارم is the present 'I have' and بجنگ is an infinitival/goal phrase 'for battle' (literally 'to fight'). So the clause means 'I have him in mind for fighting' or 'I intend to fight him.'
segments.s236-g480: This clause uses two related verbs of motion: بگردش (imperative/hortative form 'go/turn about him' or 'circle him') followed by بگردم (first‑person present/subjunctive 'I turn / I will wheel') to express repeated or emphatic action. بسان + پلنگ is a simile 'like a leopard.' Together they convey 'I will wheel about him, I will turn like a leopard.'
segments.s236-g113: دلیران is the plural subject 'the brave (men)'. پیاده شدند is a past-tense verbal phrase meaning literally 'became on-foot' = 'dismounted/went on foot' (پیاده used predicatively with شدند). آن زمان is a temporal phrase 'at that time' placed after the verb for poetic word order.
segments.s236-g114: A nominal list of equipment: سپرهای (plural 'shields') modified by the adjective چینی ('Chinese') which follows the noun (normal Persian adjective order), then the coordinated تیر و کمان ('arrows and bow'). The conjunction و simply links the items; the verb implied is carried from the previous clause ('they [had/wore/carry]').
segments.s236-g115: برفتند is a past plural verb 'they went/advanced' (here 'advanced' fits the martial context). با نیزه‌داران is a prepositional phrase 'with the spear-bearers'; بهم here is an adverb 'together' emphasizing joint action.
segments.s236-g116: بپیش functions as a directional adverb/preposition 'forward/toward' and takes اندرون 'the inner part/inner chamber' as its object; the names بیژن و گستهم follow as the possessors/occupants of that inner place. Note گستهم here is a proper name (not the verbal sense listed in the lemma data).
segments.s236-g117: دم آتش تیز is a compact image: دم ('breath/blast') of آتش ('fire') qualified by تیز ('fierce/sharp'), i.e. 'a keen/fierce blast of fire'. باران تیر is a genitival compound 'a rain (shower) of arrows'. The two coordinated images describe the attacking force (fire and arrows).
segments.s236-g118: هزیمت بود is literal past 'there was a rout/defeat'. زان سپس is a formulaic sequence 'thereupon/after that', linking cause and result, and ناگزیر is a predicate adjective 'inevitable' describing the outcome; together they indicate that rout followed and could not be avoided.
segments.s236-g119: چو is the conjunction 'when'. از is the preposition 'from'. باره here is used in a poetic/archaised sense related to a sortie/assault or burst connected to the دژ ('fortress'); together باره دژ indicates 'the (sortie/gate/assault of the) fortress' from which they came out. بیرون شدند is the past 'they went out/issued forth.'
segments.s236-g120: A doubled participle گریزان گریزان ('fleeing, fleeing') is used for emphasis: 'in flight'. بهامون is a compound-like prepositional phrase 'toward us / to us', and شدند again is the past copula 'they became/were', here giving the sense 'they turned into/appeared toward us' i.e. 'they fled toward us.'
segments.s236-g225: سخنهای کوتاه زو شد دراز = literally 'the short words of/from him became long.' سخنهای (plural noun + ـهای) = 'short words/remarks'; زو is poetic contraction of ز + او = 'from him' (indicating the source); شد is the past of شدن meaning 'became'; دراز is a predicate adjective 'long.' The structure says a brief remark turned into a long speech.
segments.s236-g226: تو با لشکری چاره او را بساز = imperative/instruction: 'You, with an army, make/arrange a remedy for him.' تو is the emphatic 2nd‑person pronoun; با + لشکری marks the instrument/means 'with an army'; چاره او را is the definite object 'a remedy for him' (او = 'him', را = object marker); بساز is the 2nd‑person singular imperative of ساختن/بسازیدن 'make/arrange.'
segments.s236-g227: سرش را ز زین اندرآور بخاک = a command: 'Bring his head from the saddle to the ground.' سرش (سر + enclitic ش) = 'his head' and is marked by را as definite object; ز زین = 'from the saddle' (ز = from, زین = saddle); اندرآور is an imperative/command form meaning 'bring down/bring in'; بخاک ('ب' + خاک) gives direction/result 'to the ground.'
segments.s236-g228: ازان پس خود از شاه ایران چه باک = 'After that, (for) oneself/ourselves, what fear of the King of Iran?' ازان پس = 'after that' or 'thereafter'; خود is emphatic/reflexive ('oneself' or 'we ourselves'); از شاه ایران = 'of/from the King of Iran' (source/concern); چه باک is a rhetorical/exclamatory phrase 'what fear?' meaning essentially 'there is no fear.'
segments.s236-g229: نه کیخسرو آباد ماند نه گنج = a paired negative 'neither... nor...' construction. نه ... ماند = 'neither did Kay Khosrow's (domain) remain/prosper' (کیخسرو آباد = Kay Khosrow's settlement/prosperity); نه گنج = 'nor (is there) treasure' (the verb is omitted second time by ellipsis).
segments.s236-g230: نداریم این زرم کردن برنج = 'We do not have this gold to make/prepare rice.' نداریم is present negative 'we do not have/possess'; این زرم = 'this gold (of mine/this gold)' (زرم here = my gold/this gold); کردن برنج = infinitival/ purpose phrase 'to make/prepare rice' (کردن used as 'to make/do' with برنج as object) — i.e. lacking money even to buy/prepare rice.
segments.s236-g231: نگه کن بدین لشکر نامدار = imperative 'Look! / Behold!' نگه کن is an imperative 'look/watch' (نگه = gaze; کن = do), بدین is the preposition/ demonstrative combination 'به + این' = 'at/this/these,' and لشکر نامدار = 'renowned army' (لشکر = army, نامدار = distinguished/renowned) with the adjective following the noun in the classical order.
segments.s236-g232: جوانان و شایسته کارزار = a noun phrase describing the army: 'young men and worthy/fit for battle.' جوانان = 'young men'; و = 'and' connecting two descriptive terms; شایسته کارزار = 'worthy/fit for battle' where شایسته modifies an understood quality with کارزار ('battle') specifying the domain.
segments.s236-g161: فرستاد is a 3rd‑person past verb ('he sent' / 'he dispatched'). یک نیمه is the noun phrase 'one half' (literal 'one half') serving as the thing sent. نزدیک شاه is a locative phrase 'near the king' (نزدیک = 'near', followed by the noun شاه 'king'); the whole segment therefore says who/what was sent and where they were placed.
segments.s236-g162: ببخشید here is a past verb meaning 'gave'/'bestowed' (an older poetic use where بخشیدن can mean to grant/assign). دیگر همه = 'the others / all the rest' (دیگر = other/remaining, همه = all). بر سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'to / upon the army' (بر + سپاه), so the clause means he assigned the remaining men to the army.
segments.s236-g163: وزان پس is a temporal connector 'and from that / and thereafter' (وزان = 'from that', پس = 'then/after'). چو here functions as the subordinating word 'when' introducing a clause of time. The following proper names (گودرز، طوس، گیو) are coordinated by و 'and' — the segment lists the heroes whose action or presence is about to be described.
segments.s236-g164: The initial چو again gives 'when'. گستهم and شیدوش appear here as coordinated names in the list; the supplied sense lists do not offer a matching proper‑name sense, so they are treated as names in this context. و is the copulative 'and' linking names. فرهاد is the proper name 'Farhad'; نیو is used here as an epithet ('ney' = reed‑flute) attached after the name in apposition. The whole segment continues the catalogue of people present.
segments.s236-g165: برخاستند is the 3rd‑person plural past 'they rose'/'they stood up' and appears at the end of the clause as the main verb. The preceding words are names coordinated as the subject (ابا, بیژن, گیو). The first item ابا is ambiguous in the supplied senses and here functions syntactically as a name or item in the subject list rather than the verbal sense 'to refuse'.
segments.s236-g166: یکی آفرین نو is a nominal phrase: یکی = 'one' (a single), آفرین = 'acclamation/praise' (here 'acclamation'), نو = 'new' modifying آفرین. آراستند is the 3rd‑person plural past 'they adorned/arranged' and governs the phrase: literally 'they arranged/made one new acclamation.' Word order places the noun phrase before the verb, as is common in Persian narrative.
segments.s236-g167: چنین is an adverb 'thus' or 'so' introducing reported speech. گفت here functions as the reporting verb 'said', but the provided sense list does not include the verbal 'said', so that sense is noted as unmatched; گودرز is the speaker's name. کای is a vocative particle 'O' used before royal titles or names, and سرفراز is an epithet 'exalted / victorious', forming the addressee of the reported speech.
segments.s236-g168: جهان را is 'the world' marked by the definite/object marker را (here topical/emphatic). بمهر means 'with love' (بـِ + مهر) and functions adverbially; تو is the 2nd‑person pronoun 'you' (the addressee). آمد is the 3rd‑person past 'came' and نیــاز is the noun 'supplication / plea' — together آمد نیاز = 'a supplication came' (i.e. 'a plea has arisen'), so the clause states that a plea has come to the world out of love for you.
segments.s236-g449: This clause is compacted poetic word order: object/verb precede the subject. ببر is a noun 'tiger' (agent), در here functions as the preposition 'in/inside' or locative marker, and گرفتش is the past tense verb (گرفت + ـش = 'seized him'). جهاندیده مرد is a nominal phrase 'worldly‑wise man' that, by position after the verb, is appositive/subject or a following topical noun referring to the person affected; the clause as a whole reads 'the tiger seized him / the worldly‑wise man'.
segments.s236-g450: ز introduces the source/reasonal phrase 'from / concerning'; کار گذشته is a genitival/Noun+adjective pairing 'past deed(s)/what has gone by'; بسی is an adverb meaning 'much' or 'a great deal' (here intensifying یاد); یاد کرد is a light‑verb construction where یاد (memory/remembrance) is the noun and کرد (past of کردن) makes the verbal meaning 'recalled/remembered'.
segments.s236-g451: بگفت is simple past 'he said' (ب + گفت, poetic). آنک introduces a relative/interrogative 'that / who'; تیمار ترکان is a possessive/agent phrase 'the care/charge of the Turks' (تیمار + ترکان). ز کی است compresses to 'of whom is it' or 'whose is ...' with ز marking source/possession and کی = 'who'; ست is the copula 'is'. The whole segment is an interrogative embedded after 'he said'.
segments.s236-g452: سرانجام is an adverb/phrase 'in the end / ultimately' or 'the outcome'; درمان is a noun 'remedy/cure' and این کار is 'this affair/matter' (demonstrative + noun). چی + ست forms a direct question 'what is ...?'; the clause asks for the ultimate remedy to the present problem. Word order places adverbial fronted for emphasis.
segments.s236-g453: خرامان is an adverbial/preterit participial adverb meaning 'with a stately gait / proudly'. بایوان is an archaic/uncertain form (textual/poetic) here functioning adverbially or as a prepositional phrase indicating direction/toward the court; خسرو is the noun 'king' (object of motion or locus), and شدند is the past plural 'they became / they went / they arrived' used as the verbal predicate. The plural verbal form locates a group action directed at the king.
segments.s236-g454: برای here provides a purposive/reasonal nuance 'for / with regard to' (or 'therefore'), و is the coordinating conjunction 'and', باندیشه is the prepositional‑style compound 'with thought / in intention', نو modifies that noun 'new', and شدند (past plural) again serves as the main verb 'they became / were moved' — together meaning 'and they adopted a new plan/intention'.
segments.s236-g455: سخن here is the object 'speech/words' and راند is the past tense 'drove/caused' used idiomatically with سخن to mean 'uttered / spoke'. هر گونه is a distributive phrase 'every kind/sort of', and افراسیاب is the proper name (subject). The verb راند preceding the subject is a common poetic word order (verb before noun).
segments.s236-g456: ز marks topics/relation 'concerning / about' (poetic use). کار درنگ is a genitive phrase 'the matter of delay' (کار = affair, درنگ = delay). و connects clauses, then ز بهر شتاب means 'and concerning/for the sake of haste' (بهر = 'for / on account of', شتاب = 'haste'). Together these adverbial phrases specify the subjects of what Afrasiab spoke about.
segments.s236-g401: بگویش is the imperative/encourage form (بگو + enclitic -یش) meaning 'tell him/it to him'. که introduces a reported clause ('that' / 'that is'), so the sequence بگویش که = 'tell him that...'. ما را uses را as the object/experiencer marker ('us' as the one affected), and چه آمد بسر is an idiomatic past construction: چه 'what', آمد (past of آمدن) 'came/happened', + بسر (here part of the light verbal phrase آمد بسر = 'befell/occurred to'). Together: 'Tell him that what has happened to us...'
segments.s236-g402: ازین is 'از + این' ('from/on account of this' or 'because of this'); نامور is an epithet meaning 'famed/renowned'; گرد here names a band/circle (poetic use: 'round / group'), and پرخاشخر is an adjectival epithet 'quarrelsome/hostile'. The whole phrase modifies the cause/agent of what happened: 'from/through this famed band of quarrelsome men.'
segments.s236-g403: اگر is the conditional 'if'. یارمندست is a fused form of یارمند است ('is supported / has allies'); note the verb (است) is attached to the adjective. چرخ بلند is the subject/complement: چرخ = 'the sky/heaven' (poetic for fate) + بلند 'high/lofty'. The clause reads 'if the lofty heaven is (or is favorable / has allies)'.
segments.s236-g404: بیاید is an optative/subjunctive third‑person form meaning 'may he come' or 'let him come'. بدین here represents به + این ('to this'), with ب- as the preposition 'to/into' and دین (orthography here) standing for 'این' ('this'); دشت is the locative noun 'plain', and پولادوند is a personal/epithetial name or epithet attached to the place/person. The phrase is the result clause: 'may he come into this plain, Puladvand.'
segments.s236-g405: بسی before a noun is a frequent poetic quantifier 'many / much' (archaic here). لشکر is the noun 'army/host' and از مرز ... is a prepositional phrase 'from the border(s) of ...', listing origins. چین here refers to 'China' (a geographical border). The whole phrase sets up 'many armies from the borders of S... and China'.
segments.s236-g406: نگونسار and حیران are predicative adjectives 'woeful/mournful' and 'astonished/perplexed'. شدند is the past plural of شدن 'became', marking the change of state, and اندرین (= ان در این) is a locative/adverbial 'in these (places / circumstances)'. Together: 'became woeful and astonished here/among them.'
segments.s236-g407: سپاهست is the fused form of سپاه است ('it/there is an army' or 'it was a host') used as a copular clause. برسان کوه روان is a poetic nominal group: کوه روان 'a moving/flowing mountain' (an epithet for tremendous mass or force), with برسان functioning adjectivally/verbally as part of the attributive phrase ('that moves/that brings/that bears'); taken together the phrase characterizes the host as 'a moving mountain' (i.e., immense, advancing like a mountain).
segments.s236-g408: سپهدارشان is 'their commander' (سپهدار + possessive -شان). This is followed appositionally by the name and title رستم پهلوان: 'Rostam the champion'. The noun phrase names the leader of that great host.
segments.s236-g273: بشیده is a proper name (here the speaker) placed before the reporting verb; چنین گفت is a fixed phrase 'thus said' where چنین ('thus') modifies the verb گفت ('said'). کای is a vocative 'O (Kay)' addressing the king, and پر خرد is an adjectival phrase 'full of wisdom' with پر (lit. 'full (of)') modifying the noun خرد ('wisdom'). Word order is name — 'thus said' — vocative phrase.
segments.s236-g274: سپاه تو تیمار تو lists two possessions ('your army, your care') with تو as the possessive/emphatic pronoun attached; کی introduces the question ('who'), and خورد is an older/past-form verb here meaning roughly 'befalls/attains' or 'takes charge of' in context — so the clause asks who will be responsible for or who will take charge of your army and your care. The verb follows the objects in this classical word order.
segments.s236-g275: چنین دان is an imperative/admonitive: دان (from دانستن) used as 'know' or 'consider' with چنین ('thus/this') — 'know this'. که introduces the following subordinate clause. این لشکر بی‌شمار is a noun phrase 'this army/host, countless', where بی‌شمار is a postpositive adjective 'countless'.
segments.s236-g276: که is the relative/that-clause marker ('which/that'); آمد is the past tense 'has come'. برین is a fused prepositional + demonstrative ('بر' + 'این' → 'to/against this') taking مرز ('frontier, border') as its object. چندین هزار is a quantifying phrase 'many thousands' placed after the noun phrase to indicate number.
segments.s236-g277: سپهدارشان is the noun 'commander' with the possessive suffix -شان 'their' attached ('their commander'). رستم is in apposition naming him, and شیر دل is a compound epithet 'lion‑hearted' (شیر 'lion' + دل 'heart') following the name as a descriptive phrase.
segments.s236-g278: که is the relative 'who/that' introducing a defining clause. از خاک is a prepositional phrase 'from the earth' (از + خاک). سازد is a present/simple-form verb 'makes/creates'. بشمشیر is an instrumental phrase built from the preposition بـ + شمشیر meaning 'with (his) sword', and گل is the direct object 'a flower' — literally 'who makes from the earth, with his sword, a flower' (a poetic metaphor).
segments.s236-g279: گو is an imperative verb 'say' or a quotative 'call (him)'. پیلتن is a name/place used as the form of address to be uttered. رستم زابلیست places the hero in apposition and origin: 'Rostam is of Zabul' (زابلیست = 'a Zabulian'). The sequence is imperative 'say/utter (Piltan)', then identification of Rostam.
segments.s236-g280: ببین is the imperative 'see/look'. تا introduces purpose/result here ('so that/that') and مر is an older/dialectal form meaning 'to me' (like مرا). او را is the definite object 'him'. هم‌آورد is used in the sense 'be his equal' or 'match him' (literally 'bring together as equal'), and کیست is an interrogative copular clause ('who is [it]') with کی 'who' + ست copula 'is'. The whole clause requests that the addressee find out and tell the speaker who can equal Rostam.
segments.s236-g249: برو is an imperative/interjection meaning “go” or “onward,” placed first as a rallying cry. سرکشان here is the group being addressed or described (an epithet in the nominative), followed by the exclamation noun آفرین (“bravo”); خواندند is a 3rd-person plural past-tense verb “they said/sang/uttered,” so the clause reads literally “They uttered ‘buru… afarin.’” Note the verb-final order and the use of آفرین as an uttered acclamation.
segments.s236-g250: سرافراز را uses را as the definite-object/contrast marker attached to the person named Srafraz (the one being called). سوی کین is an adverbial/prepositional phrase (سوی “toward” + کین “vengeance”) indicating direction/aim of the summons. خواندند is again the 3rd-person plural past verb “they called/summoned,” so the clause means “they called Srafraz toward vengeance.”
segments.s236-g251: که introduces a wished-for clause (optative sense: “may/that…”). The adjectives/epithets جاوید, شادان, پیروز are coordinated by و and form a predicate set describing the wish. باش is the present imperative/subjunctive ‘be’ used here as an optative: “may you be ….” The implied subject is 2nd person (you) carried by باش.
segments.s236-g252: بکام دلت is a genitival/possessive phrase: بکام “to the liking/pleasure” + دلت “your heart” (دل + 2nd-person suffix ت). گیتی افروز is a compound epithet (گیتی “world” + افروز “illuminating”); together with the final باش (imperative/subjunctive) it forms a second wish: “may you be pleasing to your heart and world-illumining.” Note again the imperative باش as optative and the enclitic ت marking the addressee.
segments.s236-g253: سپهبد is the subject (a military title). بسی before the plural جنگها functions as a quantifier (“many” / “much” in sense here), and جنگها is the plural object “battles.” دیده بود is the compound perfect/pluperfect (past participle + بود), literally “had seen.” Word order places the quantifier + object before the participial predicate.
segments.s236-g254: ز هر کار is a prepositional phrase “from/of every affair” (ز = “from/of,” هر = “every,” کار = “affair/task”). بهری here indicates the best/most suitable choice for each matter and works with پسندیده بود (past participle + بود) to mean “he had chosen/approved the best.” پسندیده is a past participle/adjective “chosen/approved” and بود is the past auxiliary “was/had.”
segments.s236-g255: یکی is an indefinite introducer “one / a certain,” here introducing a person. شیر دل is a compound epithet “lion‑hearted” (شیر “lion” + دل “hearted”), followed by the copula بود “was” in the past. فرغار نام places the proper name after the description: فرغار is the name (proper noun) and نام “name” marks it as such, a common Persian order “X named Y.”
segments.s236-g256: قفس دیده uses دیده (past participle “having seen”) with the object قفس “cage,” then و + جسته pairs the participle with the perfect/preterite verb جسته “he leapt/sprung.” چندی here means “for a while/somewhat,” and ز دام is the prepositional phrase “from the trap/snare.” The sequence expresses a completed action: on seeing the cage he sprang out of the snare for a time.
segments.s236-g97: The clause is compact: the first item (ابا) is ambiguous here (see senses). مغز پیکان is a genitival phrase 'the core/shaft of the arrow' (مغز + noun). همی is the frequentative/adverb 'still/kept on' placed before the verb. راز گفت literally 'said a secret' = 'revealed/whispered a secret'; گفت functions as the past verb 'said'. Word order is topic (instrument/thing) → adverb → object → verb, common in poetic Persian.
segments.s236-g98: ببدسازگاری is a nominal compound meaning 'bad compatibility / incompatibility' that modifies the following verb phrase. همی گشت جفت = 'kept becoming / became a pair' (گشت = 'became'); here the structure means 'they became a mismatched pair' — همی is again the frequentative/adverb and جفت is the resulting predicate noun 'pair'. The verb گشت is copulative ('became').
segments.s236-g99: بن باره زان پس is a temporal/causal fronting: بن (foundation/base) + باره ('occasion' here) with زان پس 'from that then/after that'. بکندن is the infinitive/masdar 'to pull out / to detach', and گرفت here is an inceptive auxiliary meaning 'took to / began to' (classical Persian constructionِ گرفتن + infinitive = 'to begin to ...').
segments.s236-g100: ز دیوار مردم فگندن گرفت: the preposition ز 'from' introduces the source (دیوار). مردم is the direct object 'people'. فگندن is the infinitive/verb 'to throw/cast down' and گرفت again functions as the inceptive auxiliary 'began to', so the whole clause reads 'he began to throw people from the wall'. The order is source → object → verb (infinitive) → auxiliary.
segments.s236-g101: ستونها نهادند is straightforward past plural 'they placed/set pillars'. زیر اندرش is a locative phrase: زیر 'under/beneath' + اندر ش (اندر = 'in/within', ش = enclitic pronoun 'it/its') giving 'beneath it' or 'under its interior'. Word order: object (ستونها) → verb → place phrase.
segments.s236-g102: This clause describes a happening: بیالود modifies the noun phrase that follows (here used predicatively/qualitatively in poetic register). نفط سیاه 'black oil' is the substance; از برش 'from its side' (از = 'from', برش here 'its side/section'). Together: 'black oil (lamp‑oil) came/ran from its side' — the verb is omitted or expressed by the copular sense of بیالود in context.
segments.s236-g103: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. نیمی ز دیوار = 'half of the wall' (نیمی = 'half' + ز = 'of/from' + دیوار = 'wall'). دژکنده شد is a past/passive-like verb phrase: دژکنده functions as a participial/past form 'broken/dug away' and شد is the auxiliary 'became/was'. So the clause reads 'when half of the wall was broken away'.
segments.s236-g104: بچوب here refers to the wooden parts or 'the timber' (the form is compact/archaic). اندر آتش = 'into/in the fire'. پراگنده شد is a verbal phrase 'was scattered' (پراگنده = scattered/widespread + شد = became/was). The clause therefore says that the wood was scattered and ended up in the fire (i.e., the timber was scattered and caught fire).
segments.s236-g241: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' placed before the phrase. بوم نیاکان is a genitive/compound meaning 'the native soil of the ancestors' (būm + niākān). وز here is an archaic contracted form (written as one word) functioning like و + ز to coordinate the two sources ('and from'); شهر خویش is a noun + possessive clitic (خوی + -ش 'his/its') meaning 'his own city'. Word order: preposition + noun phrase, then coordinated second source.
segments.s236-g242: یکی تازه اندیشه is an indefinite noun phrase 'a certain/fresh thought' (یکی = 'a certain'). بنهاد is the past of بنهادن 'to set/put/establish' in 3rd‑person (here reported narrative). پیش is an adverb meaning 'before/previously' or 'forth' and links to بنهاد as a postpositive adverbial; together the clause means 'he put forth a fresh thought.'
segments.s236-g243: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed poetic order = 'thus gave (his) reply' (چنین 'thus', داد past 'gave', پاسخ 'answer'). که introduces the reported clause; من is the subject 'I'; ساز جنگ is a noun phrase 'the means/instruments of war' (ساز used metaphorically as 'means, device' with جنگ 'war').
segments.s236-g244: بپیش آورم is a compound verbal idea: بپیش (forward) + آورم (1st‑sg. present/subjunctive of 'bring') used here in reported speech to express future/intention 'I will bring forward/prepare'. چون functions as a conditional/temporal 'when/if' (classical usage), شود کار تنگ = 'the affair becomes tight/difficult' (شود 'become', کار 'matter', تنگ 'narrow/difficult').
segments.s236-g245: نمانم is the negative present/subjunctive form (ن + مانم) meaning 'I will not remain/wait' — the negation is prefixed. که introduces the subordinate clause; کیخسرو is the proper name 'Kay Khosrow'; از تخت خویش is a prepositional phrase 'from/of his throne' (از + تخت + possessive خویش with clitic ش). The verb for the subordinate clause comes in the next segment.
segments.s236-g246: شود شاد is 'becomes glad/happy' (شود 'become', شاد 'happy'). The conjunction و connects the coordinated clause 'پدرام از بخت خویش' where the verb 'شود شاد' is ellipted and understood to apply to پدرام as well — a common coordination: 'Kay Khosrow becomes glad ... and Pedram (becomes) glad with his fortune.'
segments.s236-g247: This is a noun phrase with object marking: سر زابلی را = 'the head of the Zabulian' (سر 'head', زابلی 'a man of Zabul', را object marker). بروز نبرد is a temporal/occasional phrase 'on the day of battle' (بروز 'on the day/occasion', نبرد 'battle') that modifies the whole action.
segments.s236-g248: This is an archaic, inverted verbal phrase meaning 'I will place/bring into my long grasp.' بچنگ = 'into the grasp' (ب + چنگ), دراز = 'long', اندر = 'in/into' (classical post/prepositional particle). آرم and بگرد are older/poetic verb forms here combining to express the speaker's action/intention; word order is inverted compared with modern colloquial Persian, so these verbs come after their object and adverbials.
segments.s236-g433: A coordinated noun phrase: اسیران بسیار = “many captives” (noun + quantifier following it), joined by و to پیلان رمه = “a herd of elephants.” رمه is a collective noun (herd/flock) attached after پیلان without an ezāfe, a poetic condensation common in Ferdowsi.
segments.s236-g434: فرستاد is the simple past 3rd singular 'he sent/dispatched.' یکسر is an adverb modifying the verb meaning 'at once/altogether.' بایران functions as a locative/directional phrase 'to the barren plains' (bāyrān = barren), and همه reinforces that the sending applied to all of them. Word order places the object phrases before the verb, a common Persian pattern, especially in poetry.
segments.s236-g435: کنارنگ و جنگ‌آوران را is a coordinated direct object marked by the object marker را; the verb بخواند (past 3sg) here means 'he called/summoned' (or 'had them perform/recite'), with the prefixed b- forming the classical past. The را marks these groups as the ones being summoned.
segments.s236-g436: ز هر گونه‌ای داستانها is a prepositional phrase 'from/of every kind the stories' (ز = 'from/of' in poetic register; هر گونه‌ای = 'every kind'), followed by the verb براند (past 3sg from راند) which literally means 'drove' but here is used idiomatically to mean 'set forth/put forth/related'—i.e. 'told/ran through tales.' The word order (prepositional phrase + noun + verb) is typical of the epic style.
segments.s236-g437: بدیشان is a compound pronoun phrase 'to them/for them.' بگفت is the past third‑person 'he said/told.' انچ is a relative pronoun 'that which/what,' and در نامه بود is a subordinate clause 'was in the letter' with بود as the past copula. The clause overall means 'he told them what was in the letter.'
segments.s236-g438: This is a predicative clause: جهانگیر is a proper name/title, followed by two adjectival predicates برنا (youthful/vigorous) and خودکامه (autocratic) joined by و, with the copula بود 'was' at the end. The order is Name + qualities + copula, giving 'Jahan‑gir was young and autocratic.'
segments.s236-g439: بفرمود is an honorific past 'he commanded/ordered.' تا introduces a purpose or command clause and is followed by the subjunctive/jussive برند ('they bring/take'), so تا + برند expresses 'that they should bring.' کوس بیرون برند = 'bring the war-drum out.' The verb at the end and the subordinate clause introduced by تا are typical of classical narrative style.
segments.s236-g440: This clause parallels the previous: سراپرده او = 'his pavilion/inner tent' (possessive pronoun following the noun), followed by the directional phrase به هامون 'to the plain,' and the same verb برند 'bring/take' completing the commanded action. The structure shows two coordinated commands introduced by the initial بفرمود تا.
segments.s236-g89: This is a compact nominal juxtaposition describing two directions/faces. بِیک روی (here written بیک روی) means “one face/side” (classical contraction of بهِ یک روی) followed by the addressee: گودرز. The structure بیک روی ... و یک روی ... is parallel: 'one face toward X and one face toward Y.' روی is used in the sense 'face/side' and the conjunction و links the two parallel phrases.
segments.s236-g90: A short adverbial/nominal clause describing what follows them. پس here = 'after/behind' (temporal/spatial), followed by پشت او 'his/their rear' (پشت + pronoun او). پیل is the noun 'elephant' as the following actor/thing, and با بوق و کوس is an instrumental phrase 'with a horn and a drum' (با + instruments). Word order is adverbial phrase → locative (پشت او) → noun → instrument.
segments.s236-g91: Parallel in form to the previous 'one face' phrase: بیک روی = 'one side/one face', بر لشکر زابلی = 'toward / against the Zabli army' where بر functions as the preposition 'toward/against' governing لشکر. The phrase names the direction or opponent that one of the faces/units is turned to.
segments.s236-g92: A compact noun phrase describing an armed man: زره‌دار is an 'armored (warrior/horseman)' used as the head noun; با introduces the instrument/possessed weapon 'with a dagger' (خنجر). کابلی is an adjective meaning 'of Kabul / Kabul‑born' qualifying the dagger (i.e., a Kabul-style dagger). No overt verb — a descriptive tag for that fighter.
segments.s236-g93: A two-clause sequence: چو آن دید رستم = 'when Rostam saw that' (چو introducing a temporal subordinate clause), followed by کمان برگرفت = 'he raised / took up (his) bow.' برگرفت is the past perfective verb 'took up/raised.' The subject 'he' is explicitly given as رستم in the subordinate clause and remains the implied subject of the main clause (hence no separate pronoun before برگرفت).
segments.s236-g94: Descriptive verbless/resultative clause with ماند as the stative verb 'remained / was left': همه دژ = 'the whole fortress' (subject), بدو = dative 'toward him / before him', ماند = 'remained / was left', اندر شگفت = 'in wonder / astonishment' (اندر + noun as adverbial). Together: 'The entire citadel was left in wonder at him.'
segments.s236-g95: Relative clause begun by هر آنکس که = 'whoever' / 'whomever' (هر + آنکس + که). از باره sets the context 'from [in] the engagement / in the fray' (باره here 'occasion / instance, the particular combat'), and سر بر زدی is a verbal phrase where سر + بر + زدی means 'you struck (off) the head' (بر here is a separable/localizing element meaning 'off / at the head' and زدی is the past tense 'you struck/attacked'). The clause expresses a general conditional: 'whoever you strike the head of in battle...'.
segments.s236-g96: Main result clause with زمانه personified as agent: زمانه = 'time / fate' (here = fate that avenges events). سرش را is the definite object 'his head' (ش = his, را = object marker). بهم (به‌هم) is an adverbial/idiomatic particle meaning 'together / to pieces / in turn' modifying the verb, and در زدی (در + زدی) is the verbal element 'you (or one) strike / cause to be struck' used idiomatically to express that fate will do the same to him. The idiom as a whole means that fate will reciprocate the beheading — poetic personification of retribution.
segments.s236-g209: بپیچید is a 3rd-person past/preterite verb (literary/poetic form) meaning ‘he turned’ or ‘he turned aside’. وزان is a contracted poetic form (از + آن) meaning ‘from that / thereupon’; پس is a connective adverb ‘then/thereupon’; به ... گفت is a light prepositional phrase + past verb where به + آواز (‘with voice / aloud’) modifies گفت (‘said/spoke’). Together the segment gives the action (‘he turned away and then spoke aloud’).
segments.s236-g210: The initial که introduces the reported content (a declarative subordinate clause: ‘that/which’). با او is a simple prepositional phrase ‘with him’. The second که here functions as a relative/introducing conjunction linking داریم (‘we have’) to its object (i.e. ‘whom we have’). داریم is the present/factual verb ‘we have/possess’; در جنگ is a locative prepositional phrase ‘in/for battle’; جفت is a noun meaning ‘match / pair’. Altogether this clause asks/declares who is the army’s match in battle.
segments.s236-g211: یکی is the indefinite ‘one / a certain’, here introducing someone; کودکی is the noun ‘a child / youngster’ (used as a nominative); بود is the past copula ‘there was’. برسان is an infinitival/agentive form from رساندن meaning ‘bringing / bearing’ (here describing the child as bearing/bringing something); نی is the instrument noun ‘ney / reed (flute)’. The word order is typical literary Persian: determiner + noun + copula, then a participial descriptor for what the child carried.
segments.s236-g212: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause ‘whom/for whom’. من is the first-person pronoun ‘I’. لشکر is the direct object ‘army/host’. آورده is the perfective participle ‘brought’ and بودم is the past-tense auxiliary ‘I had / I was’, so together آورده بودم = ‘I had brought’. بری is an adjectival/locative word attached at the end (archaic/poetic); here it modifies the situation (its exact older sense is context-dependent). The clause as a whole says ‘for whom I had brought the army’ (literally ‘that I had brought an army for’).
segments.s236-g213: بیامد is a past verb ‘came’ (literary); تن من is the noun phrase ‘my body’ (object/subject depending on clause); ز is the poetic preposition ‘from’; زین is the noun ‘saddle’; برگرفت is a past verb compound (بر + گرفت) meaning ‘took up / seized / lifted’. The sequence lays out successive actions: ‘my body came from the saddle’ followed by ‘(someone) took it up’ — typical compressed narrative style in epic Persian.
segments.s236-g214: فرو is an adverb/particle meaning ‘down / downward / wholly’; ماند is the past of ماندن ‘remained / was left’. زان is the contracted poetic form of از + آن ‘from that / thereupon’. لشکر is ‘the army’; اندر is an older locative preposition meaning ‘in / among’; شگفت is a nominal/adjectival word ‘wonder / amazement’. Together the clause says that the army remained amazed (i.e. ‘the army stood in wonder’).
segments.s236-g215: چنین is an adverb meaning ‘thus / so’; گفت is the past verb ‘said’ (here used with a human agent); لشکر is the subject ‘the army/host’; بافراسیاب is a prepositional compound meaning ‘to/with Afrasiab’ (با + فراسیاب, addressed to Afrasiab). Grammatically this is the speech-tag introducing reported speech (‘thus the army said to Afrasiab’).
segments.s236-g216: The initial که again introduces the content of what was said (a subordinate clause). چندین is the indefinite numeral/adjective ‘several / many a’; سر is the noun ‘head(s)’ used literally (many heads, i.e. casualties) or figuratively; از is the preposition ‘from / of’; جنگ is ‘war / battle’ and رستم is the proper name ‘Rostam’ in the genitive (i.e. ‘Rostam’s battle’). متاب is a present/stative or imperative-form verb from a classical root meaning roughly ‘be agitated / be moved’ (literary usage). The clause provides the substance of the army’s remark about the battle with Rostam.
segments.s236-g417: من is the first-person subject 'I'. از introduces the source or domain ('from/out of'). پادشاهی آباد is a noun + adjective sequence 'prosperous kingdom' (noun before its adjective here is poetic). خوی + ش is the possessive unit 'خویش' = 'my/one's own'; in the text it is split into خوی (root) and the clitic ش (his/her/its), so the whole phrase means 'from my prosperous kingdom'.
segments.s236-g418: نه negates the verb that follows. برگیرم is first‑person-singular present/subjunctive (here translated as a present/future refusal) 'I take / I will take' (negative = 'I will not take'). از governs the following noun phrase 'from hardship'. The doubled رنج is poetic repetition ('رنج یک رنج' = 'one single hardship'); بیش is an adverb 'more' — together the phrase means 'not take from (it) even one more hardship'.
segments.s236-g419: دگر 'the other' modifies نیمه 'half'. دیهیم is an archaic/poetic form here; the manuscript glosses do not supply a matching modern sense, so it functions elliptically as a verbal/possessive idea ('(the) other half (we) give / (the) other half is (for) us/you') — the exact nuance is poetic and context‑dependent. و links to the following clause. گنج آن تست: گنج 'treasure/wealth', آن 'its/the', and تست is an older 2nd‑person predicate form meaning 'is yours' (see next segment for the same verb form).
segments.s236-g420: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause 'for / because / that'. امروز is the temporal adverb 'today'. پیگار is an archaic/poetic word for 'battle/struggle' and is the subject of the clause. و joins 'رنج آن' ('its hardship'). تست here is the same archaic predicate form used above meaning 'is yours' (2nd‑person singular predicate); the clause as a whole says 'for today the fighting and its hardship are yours.'
segments.s236-g421: نهادند is the third‑person plural past of نهادن 'to place / to set' (they placed). بر governs the following nominal phrase ('upon'). نامه‌بر is a compound 'letter‑carrier / envoy' and is the indirect object receiving the action. مهر شاه is a genitive construction 'the king's seal' (مهر here = 'seal/stamp' rather than 'love'). Word order places the verb before the prepositional phrase, a common poetic ordering.
segments.s236-g422: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. برزد is an archaic past verb used poetically meaning something like 'rose/appeared/issued' (context shows emergence of the 'head' from a sign). سر here means 'head' or 'summit' (the referent is the head/crest of the moon or an astrological point). از برج خرچنگ ماه is a prepositional phrase 'from the tower/sign of the Crab (Cancer) of the moon' — an astrological image; note the adjective ordering and the archaic noun forms.
segments.s236-g423: کمر بست is a fixed verbal phrase 'girded the waist / girded oneself' (past tense: she girded). شیده is the feminine/epithet 'shideh' meaning 'love‑struck' or the personal name Shideh; it serves as the subject here. ز پیش پدر is a prepositional phrase 'in front of / before the father' (ز = poetic 'from/before', پیش = 'presence of'). The verb precedes its subject in this poetic ordering but the sense is 'Shideh girded (herself) before her father.'
segments.s236-g424: فرستاده is 'messenger' (can also mean 'sent one'). او is the pronoun 'he/she' referring back to a person in context. بود is the past copula 'was'. The phrase و تیمار بر is elliptical: و 'and', تیمار 'care/charge', بر 'upon' — poetically contracted to mean 'and he assumed the care / and took charge of (it)'. Grammatically, the clause uses an omitted verb/complement after تیمار بر but the meaning is 'he was the messenger and was in charge.'
segments.s236-g441: سپاه is the grammatical subject ('the army'); انجمن شد is a compound predicate ('became an assembly' = 'assembled'). شد is a past intransitive/copular verb here. بکردار دیو is a noun phrase following the clause — بکردار is an adjectival epithet placed before دیو (poetic/archaic order) and describes that emerging figure as a 'demon' with a specified caractère; word order is inverted for poetic effect.
segments.s236-g442: برآمد is a past-tense narrative verb 'issued forth / arose'; ز introduces the source ('from') and takes the following nominal phrase گردان لشکر ('a troop/division of the army'). غریو follows as an adverbial/nominal indicating the manner — 'with a shout/clamor'. The verb-first order (برآمد ز ...) is a poetic placement but the syntactic roles remain verb + source + noun + manner.
segments.s236-g443: درفش is the head noun 'the banner/standard'; از پس و پیش is an adverbial idiom 'from back and front' meaning 'all around' or 'from every side'; پولادوند is an epithet meaning 'steel-clad/steel‑hearted' that follows to qualify the banner. This is an inverted, compact descriptive clause typical of epic diction.
segments.s236-g444: سپردار is the noun 'commander' (the agent described next). با is the preposition 'with'; ترک + ش here represent the bound form ترکش ('his quiver' / 'his arrows') split in the segmentation: ترک (stem) and ش (the clitic 'his'). The second با introduces an additional instrument/complement کمند 'lasso'. Word order lists instruments after the agent, joined by repeated با for emphasis.
segments.s236-g445: فرود آمد is a past narrative compound meaning 'descended'/'alighted' (فرود noun + آمد auxiliary-like use); از کوه is the source 'from the mountain'. و links clauses. بگذاشت is a past tense verb with the old verbal prefix بـ (here 'he set/caused/let'), and آب is its direct object 'water' — together 'he let water (flow)' or 'he put down water'.
segments.s236-g446: بیامد is another past narrative verb 'he came'; بنزدیک is a prepositional/locative expression 'near' or 'close to' (بـ + نزدیک), and افراسیاب is the proper name object of that closeness. The clause is compact: 'he came near Afrasiab'.
segments.s236-g447: یکایک سپاه is 'each (and every) unit of the army' functioning as the logical subject. پذیره شدندش is a compact phrase: پذیره (reception/accepted) with شدند (past plural) and the clitic ش = 'him'. Together they mean 'they received him' or 'were accepting him'; the verbal/clitic cluster is placed before the subject for poetic inversion.
segments.s236-g448: تبیره is 'an explanation/interpretation' (subject); برآمد is the past narrative verb 'arose/issued forth'; ز درگاه شاه is a prepositional phrase 'from the king's court/threshold' giving the source. Typical epic arrangement places the subject and verb before the source phrase for emphasis.
segments.s236-g65: پدر نام تو is a possessive noun phrase 'your father's name' (paternal noun + name + clitic you). چون + بزادی forms a temporal clause 'when/since you were born' — بزادی is an archaic verbal form related to زادن 'to be born' used here as a past/temporal marker. چه کرد is a concise rhetorical question 'what did (it) do/achieve' with کرد the simple past of کردن placed at the end as the main verb.
segments.s236-g66: This short clause names an agent and an action: کمندافگنی‌گر 'the one who casts a lasso/net' + سپهر 'the sky/heaven(s)'. نبرد here functions as the predicate; in context it yields the sense 'did not engage/contest (with)' or 'did not oppose' (an older/poetic use). Word order is noun + noun + verb (SOV style typical of Persian poetry).
segments.s236-g67: دریغست is a frozen predicative phrase 'it is a pity/it is regrettable' (دریغ + است contracted). رنج اندرین شارستان is a noun phrase 'hardship in this town' where اندرین = 'in this' modifies شارستان 'town/city'. The whole line is a verbless clause headed by دریغست.
segments.s236-g68: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'which/that'. داننده 'the knower/expert' is the subject, خواند is a past-tense verb 'called/summarized' (here 'called/named'), and ورا is the object pronoun 'it/him' (referring back to the hardship/town). کارستان is the predicate complement 'a feat/marvel'. The clause reads literally 'which a knower called it a feat.'
segments.s236-g69: چو here is temporal 'when'. The phrase places the patient/name (تور) before the subject and verb in a poetic inversion; the intended reading is 'When Fereydun drove Tur from Iran' with فریدون as subject and براند (past of راندن) as verb 'drove away'. ز ایران is a prepositional phrase 'from Iran.'
segments.s236-g70: ز هر گونه introduces a partitive/collective 'from every kind/sort'. دانندگان را بخواند is 'he summoned the knowers' — را marks the definite object and بخواند (from خواندن in this sense) means 'to call/summon' in past tense, so the whole clause means 'he called/summoned knowers of every kind.'
segments.s236-g71: یکی باره is an adverbial phrase 'all at once/at one time'. افگند is the past 'he threw/cast'. زین گونه پی is a manner phrase 'in this way/after this fashion' — گونه here = 'manner' and پی = 'after/following' (پی can indicate manner or succession); زین as written in the provided lexicon list is not the demonstrative here, so note: the construction functions adverbially to describe how he cast.
segments.s236-g72: A simple enumerative list introduced by repeated ز 'from/of': 'from stone and from brick and from wood and from reed.' The conjunction و 'and' links the items; each noun names a material (سنگ, خشت, چوب, نی). The sequence is the direct object/complements of افگند (what was cast).
segments.s236-g305: This is a coordinated negative clause: ز (a poetic preposition = “from/of”) introduces its object (ز نیزه = “from the spear”), and the verb نترسد is third‑person singular present with the negative prefix ن‑, meaning “he does not fear.” The construction نه ... نه ... is used to list negatives: the first negative is carried by نترسد, and the following نه introduces the second negated item (نه از تیغ تیز). Note the noun+adjective order تیغ تیز (noun followed by adjective).
segments.s236-g306: برآرد is a third‑person singular verb (classical/poetic form of برآورد) meaning “brings forth/raises.” ز دشمن (preposition + noun) marks the source/target (“from the enemy” or “against the enemy”), and همی is an archaic/prosodic prefix indicating continuous or habitual action (presentive particle). رستخیز is the noun object ‘rising/uprising’ (here: a muster/uprising), so the clause reads literally “(he) brings forth from the enemy an uprising.”
segments.s236-g307: تو گفتی که is the reporting frame: گفتی is second‑person past (“you said”) and که introduces the reported clause. از marks a source or cause; روی is the grammatical subject (‘face/visage’), and آهنست is the fused predicative form (آهن + است) meaning “is (made) of iron.” The segment uses the classical copular form where the predicate follows the subject; وز here modifies or explains cause/quality of the روی (see senses).
segments.s236-g308: This is a negative contrast clause introduced by نه (“not”): مردم نژادست is literally “(he) is of the people's lineage” where نژادست is a predicative compound (نژاد + است). The following کهرمنست supplies the contrasting predicate (“(he) is kahreman/amber‑made”), so the structure functions like English “not X but Y.” The copular endings are classical/poetic (‑ست).
segments.s236-g309: سلیحست is a poetic fused copula (سلیح + است) meaning “is armed/armored.” چندان is a degree adverb “so much/to that extent.” برو روز is an archaic locative phrase meaning “on that day” (برو is an old prepositional/adverbial form related to بر + او), and کین is the modifying noun “vengeance” — together روز کین = “the day of vengeance.” The predicative follows in classical order and the whole phrase gives a temporal/degree frame for the following result clause.
segments.s236-g310: که introduces a result/explanatory clause (“so that/that”), سیر آمد is a compound past meaning literally “came sated/filled” (سیر + آمد). از marks source/agent of filling (“from/by the”), بار is the noun “load/burden,” and پشت زمین is a genitive phrase “the back of the earth” (پشت modifies زمین). Together the clause states the consequence: the earth’s back was made full by the load.
segments.s236-g311: زره دارد is a simple existential/possessive clause (“he has armor”), with دارد as the present third‑person auxiliary/verb “has.” The conjunction و links a list (زره و جوشن و خود و گبر): جوشن is another word for cuirass/armor, خود is the reflexive/emphatic pronoun “himself,” and گبر is a noun (here likely an epithet ‘gabr’). In poetry such a string may pack apparatus and identity together; syntactically the verb دارد governs the whole coordinated list.
segments.s236-g312: This segment is compact and poetic: بغرد appears as a third‑person verb form (poetic/rare in modern Persian) describing a loud utterance or sound; بکردار likely qualifies manner or agency (literally ‘with/deed’ or ‘in deed’ — an adverbial/attributive form); غرنده ابر is a noun phrase “roaring cloud” (غرنده = ‘roaring’, ابر = ‘cloud’). The word order is compressed and archaic: verb + adverbial/qualifier + descriptive noun phrase; semantically it likens the subject’s action to a roaring cloud. Several items here are poetically compressed and their exact lexical identifications are archaic.
segments.s236-g329: The particle نه negates two parallel noun phrases; Persian repeats نه before each coordinated element for emphasis. آن شهر ماند = 'that city remained/lasted' (ماند is 3rd‑singular past of ماندن). The second clause repeats the pattern: نه آن شهریار = 'nor that sovereign' (elliptically continuing the verb ماند).
segments.s236-g330: سرآید is used in the sense 'to come to an end / cease' (here a future/optative sense: 'will end/come to pass'). مگر = 'except' (restrictive), so the sequence means 'it will end only except/on me' i.e. 'it will end only with me'. بر is the preposition 'upon/on', من is the first‑person object pronoun 'me' (emphatic here), این کارزار = 'this battle/conflict'.
segments.s236-g331: اگر introduces a conditional clause. دست رستم بود literally 'Rostam's hand were' — here دست stands for help/force/presence of Rostam (a metonymic use), and بود is past ('were/was'), giving a counterfactual/conditional: 'If Rostam's aid/power were [there] on the day of battle.' روز جنگ = 'the day of battle'.
segments.s236-g332: نسازم is the verb ساختن with a negative prefix ن, meaning 'I would not make/do' (here negated subjunctive/optative: 'I would not...'). من is the subject 'I' placed after the verb for emphasis. ایدر is an archaic form of 'here'. فراوان modifies درنگ meaning 'much' or 'greatly', and درنگ = 'delay/tarrying'. Word order (verb — subject — locative — adverb — noun) is poetic but grammatical in Persian.
segments.s236-g333: شوم is a first‑person subjunctive/optative ('may I become / may I be'). تا here expresses extent/purpose ('so that / until/as far as'). The sequence بدان is the contracted prepositional phrase 'به + آن' meaning 'to/at that' (here split in the word list), and روی means 'side/surface' (i.e. 'that side'). دریای چین = 'the Sea of China' (دریای = genitive 'sea of').
segments.s236-g334: بدو = 'to/at it' (referring back to that side); مانم is 1st‑person present/subjunctive of ماندن 'I remain / I would remain' (here continuing the optative sense). این مرز توران زمین is a noun phrase 'this frontier (مرز) of the land (زمین) of Turan (توران)', with زمین serving as 'land/country'.
segments.s236-g335: بدو = 'to him/unto him' (dative clitic). شیده is the speaker's name (here in apposition) and گفت is the past verb 'said' — the order 'بدو شیده گفت' places the indirect object and subject before the verb in typical epic/poetic inversion. ای introduces a vocative: ای خردمند شاه = 'O wise king' (adjective before noun in Persian vocative).
segments.s236-g336: انوشه is a proper name (Anoushe). بدی here means 'evil' or 'wrongdoing' (the clause says Anoushe committed/was evil). تا بود = 'as long as / while there was' (تا with بود meaning 'while/so long as there existed'); تاج و گاه = 'crown and realm/time' — تاج 'crown' (symbol of sovereignty) and گاه here 'occasion/place/time/seat' related to rule. Together they mean 'while crown and rule existed.'
segments.s236-g57: عمودی is an adverbial manner word ('vertically/straight down') modifying the following verb. بزد is the past 3rd-person singular of زدن ('he struck') and appears before its nominal subject (پور زال) because of poetic inversion; بر سرش is a prepositional phrase 'upon his head' (بر + سر + enclitic ش = 'his'). پور زال is the agent, a compound name meaning 'son of Zāl', functioning as the subject after the verb.
segments.s236-g58: که here introduces the result clause ('so that/and thus'). بر هم شکستش is a compact verbal idea: بر + هم (together/with) + شکست + -ش (enclitic object 'it/him') and means 'smashed/broke it to pieces'; the object suffixed to شکست names what was broken. The list سر و ترگ و یال enumerates the broken parts ('head, [torn] flesh/shred, and mane'), following the verb by poetic word order.
segments.s236-g59: چنین تا is a temporal/resultive frame 'thus, until'. در دژ 'in the fortress' is a locative phrase. یکی is an indefinite subject 'one / someone' (here 'someone'), حمله is the object 'an attack', and برد functions as the past predicate meaning 'carried out / scored' (an idiomatic use of بردن/برد indicating success), so the clause reads 'until someone mounted an attack in the fortress.'
segments.s236-g60: بزرگان is the plural subject 'the great ones/elders'. نبودند is the past negative 'they were not' (3rd-person plural). پیدا is predicate/adverb 'visible / to be seen', and ز خرد is an ablative/causal phrase 'out of prudence/wisdom' (ز = 'from/because of' used poetically). The order places the subject before the negative auxiliary and the predicate (poetic inversion): 'the elders were not seen because of prudence.'
segments.s236-g61: در دژ is locative 'in the fortress'. ببستند is the past 3rd-person plural of بستن 'they closed / they shut (up)'. وز باره تیز is a compressed nominal phrase: وز (a blast/onslaught) + باره (a burst/occasion) + تیز (sharp/quick), acting as an adverbial/qualifier for what follows — 'a swift, sharp burst/onslaught.'
segments.s236-g62: برآمد is the past 'arose/issued forth'. خروشیدن is a verbal noun/infinitive used as a substantive 'the roaring/clamor', and رستخیز ('uprising' here) specifies the kind of roar. Together the sequence means 'the roar of the uprising arose' or more idiomatically 'the clamour of the rising broke out.'
segments.s236-g63: بگفتند is the past 'they said' introducing direct speech. کای is a vocative particle 'O' (addressing someone). مرد بازور و هوش is a compound address: مرد 'man', بازور functions as an adjectival phrase 'of/with strength' (literally 'by strength' but used attributively 'mighty/strong'), and هوش 'wit/intelligence' — together: 'O man of strength and wisdom.'
segments.s236-g64: برین گونه is a fused locution (بر + این + گونه) meaning 'in this way / in this manner' (ین here is the orthographic variant of این). با ما 'with us' marks the target of the action. بکینه is an adverbial phrase 'with hatred / in enmity' (ب- + کینه). مکوش is the negative imperative of کوشیدن ('do not try / do not attempt'); together: 'do not, in this manner and with hatred, attempt hostility against us.'
segments.s236-g17: This is a nominal clause with verbless copula expressed by بود. مرین is a proper name (fronted), مرد را uses را to mark the definite noun 'the man' as topic/definite object, followed by نام 'name' and the predicate کافور; بود is the past copula 'was'. Word order: [Name] [the man-RA] [name] [Kafur] [was].
segments.s236-g18: The relative clause is introduced by که 'who/that'. او را uses را in a dative/definite role (common in older Persian: 'he had'), then بران شهر is a locative phrase 'in/at that city' and منشور 'a charter/inscription' is the predicate noun; بود is the past copula. Overall: 'who had a charter in that city.'
segments.s236-g19: بپوشید is a past action 'he put on / he clothed himself' (classical poetic past form). کافور is the person (here a proper name — not the literal camphor), followed by خفتان 'sleep' and جنگ 'war' — in context these nouns describe the guise or occasion (poetic compression). The verb governs the following nouns as its object/measure: 'Kafur put on (for) the sleep/war...' (poetic ellipsis).
segments.s236-g20: A short verbless clause: همه شهر 'the whole city / all the townspeople' is the subject, با او 'with him' is an accompanying-comitative phrase, and بسان پلنگ is a simile 'like a leopard'. The copula is omitted (typical in epic poetry): '(all) the city with him (was) like a leopard.'
segments.s236-g21: A simple coordinate nominal clause: کمندافگن 'lasso-throwers' (agent noun) and زورمندان 'the powerful/strong men' are joined by و. بدند is an archaic/poetic plural past (cf. بودند) meaning 'they were / there were', so the full sense is 'there were lasso-throwers and strong men.'
segments.s236-g22: This clause uses an archaic verb form: برزم here relates to the battlefield/field (or an archaic verb sense 'trembled/appeared') and is followed by اندرون 'within'. پیل دندان is a genitive-like grouping 'elephant's tusks' (پیل + دندان), and بدند (parallel to the previous بدند) supplies the past sense 'were shown/appeared'. So syntactically: '(In) the inner field the elephant's tusks were displayed.'
segments.s236-g23: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. گستهم is the proper name (Gostaham) used here as subject (the provided sense list's verbal sense does not apply, so treated as a name). گیتی 'world' and بران گونه 'in that way/manner' form the object/scene, and دید is the past verb 'saw'. Word order is temporal marker → subject → object phrase → verb.
segments.s236-g24: This is the main clause: جهان 'the world' (topic) followed by the locative phrase در کف دیو 'in the palm/hand of the demon', then the adjective وارونه 'upside-down' and the past verb دید 'saw'. The structure places the setting ('in the demon's hand') between topic and comment — a common poetic inversion: 'he saw the world in the demon's hand turned upside-down.'
segments.s236-g425: بکردار is an adjectival/nominal description placed before آتش to qualify it ('of a certain character'); here it functions like 'fiery in disposition'. ز introduces the reason or source ('from' / 'out of'), followed by بیم گزند 'fear of harm' (بیم = fear, گزند = harm). The whole chunk is a circumstantial phrase giving the man's state or motive.
segments.s236-g426: بیامد is the simple past 'came'. بنزدیک is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'near, close to' (poetic form with the prefixed بـ), and پولادوند is a name or epithet placed after the locative phrase as the goal/target. Word order is Subject (understood) + predicate: 'came (to) near Polādvand.'
segments.s236-g427: برو here functions as a personal name (identical in form to the verb 'go' but used as a noun/subject). آفرین کرد is a light‑verb construction where the noun آفرین ('praise, applause') combines with کرد to mean 'expressed praise' or 'praised'. و links the two verbs; نامه بداد literally reads 'gave a letter' with نامه as the object and بداد intended as past 'he gave' (in older texts written as the prefix بـ + داد).
segments.s236-g428: همه کار رستم is the definite object phrase 'all the deeds of Rostam' (کار = 'deed/work', with رستم as possessive). برو is again the subject/name. کرد یاد is another light‑verb construction: یاد ('memory/remembrance') + کرد yields 'remembered' or 'caused to be remembered' — here 'he remembered'. The clause order is Subject (برو) + Verb phrase (کرد یاد) with the object preceding the verb.
segments.s236-g429: که at the start of the clause functions temporally/causally ('when' / 'that'). رستم is the subject and بیامد is the past 'came'. ز ایران gives the origin ('from Iran'), and بجنگ is an adverbial/infinitival phrase 'to battle' or 'for fighting' (classical poetic use of the prepositional/infinitival form).
segments.s236-g430: ابا here is used in a prepositional/adverbial slot meaning essentially 'with' (a prosthetic / poetic form related to با), followed by او 'him' as the accompaniment. سپاهی is the noun 'a force / troop' and بسان پلنگ is a simile 'like a leopard' (بسان = 'like', پلنگ = 'leopard'). The sequence describes that he had with him a force likened to a leopard.
segments.s236-g431: ببند is an imperative form 'close!' that, in narrative contexts, often appears as a terse pastive/action ('he shut/closed'). اندر آورد is a compound past 'brought in' (اندر = 'into/within', آورد = 'brought'), and کاموس را is the definite object marked by را ('the Kamus' — here likely a book/title). The verb phrase sequence expresses consecutive actions: 'he shut (it), and brought in the Kamus.'
segments.s236-g432: چو begins a comparative/listing clause meaning 'as' or 'like'. خاقان و منشور و فرطوس را lists three items linked by و ('and'); را at the end is the object marker that scopes over the whole list. منشور here is a physical inscribed tablet/charter and فرطوس is an obscure or rare lexical item/name — the whole phrase reads 'like the Khāqān and the tablet and Fartus (i.e., likewise he did these).'
segments.s236-g337: ترا is the emphatic/accusative form 'to you' (ت + را) placed before the nouns for emphasis — a common poetic order meaning 'you have' or 'to you there is'. فر is the royal 'glory' (here a noun). برزست is an archaic/poetic form related to برزیدن, used nominally/adjectivally as 'exaltation, high standing' (the verb sense is backgrounded). مردانگی is a nominal quality 'manliness/valor'. The three nouns are coordinated by و and the sentence omits an explicit copula, so the list is understood predicatively about 'ترا'.
segments.s236-g338: This is a continuation of the enumerative list about 'ترا' (to you): نژاد (lineage), دل (heart), بخت (fate/fortune) and فرزانگی (wisdom). The coordinating و links each item. Word order is noun‑and‑noun typical of Persian lists; again there is no copula, the qualities are ascribed to 'ترا'.
segments.s236-g339: نباید is an impersonal modal meaning 'it should not be' / 'one ought not' (here addressed to 'you' = 'you ought not'). ترا again is the emphatic/accusative 'you' (ت + را). پند آموزگار = 'the admonition/advice of a teacher' (پند = advice, آموزگار = teacher). The structure is elliptical: 'نباید (که) ترا پند آموزگار (باشد)' — i.e. 'you should not need a teacher's counsel.'
segments.s236-g340: نگه کن is an imperative: نگه (a noun here 'watch/keeping') + کن (imperative 'do/keep') = 'keep watch' or 'heed'. بدین is the prepositional/demonstrative 'به + این' ('with/in this' / 'to this'); in the edition segmentation it appears as ب + دین. گردش روزگار is a genitive phrase 'the turning/cycle of the world/times'. Together: 'keep watch over this turning of the world' — a directive to observe the world's changes. Note the poetic elision and archaic compounds.
segments.s236-g341: چو is the comparative/simile 'like' or 'as'. A list of eminent names follows (پیران, هومان, فرشیدورد) joined by و; these are proper names/epithets invoked as examples. The clause is elliptical (no verb) and functions as 'as in the case of Pīrān...'.
segments.s236-g342: Again چو introduces a parallel list (کلباد و نستیهن). شیر مرد is a two‑word compound: شیر ('lion') modifies مرد ('man'), producing the idiom 'lion‑man' = a very brave/valiant man. Persian often places the modifier before the noun; no copula is expressed, so the phrase identifies those names as 'lion‑men.'
segments.s236-g343: شکسته and گسسته are past‑participle/adjectival forms meaning 'broken' / 'shattered'. سلیح is an archaic word for 'weapon' and دلند here is 'دل' with the 3rd‑plural ending -ند (poetic formation) meaning 'they are (of) heart' — read as 'their hearts are broken'. The two adjectival phrases are coordinated with و, describing the state of these men.
segments.s236-g344: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/because of'. بیم 'fear' and غم 'sorrow/grief' are given as causes. هر زمان ('at any time' / 'every moment') modifies the verb بگسلند, which here is present/future with the verbal prefix ب‑ marking habitual or potential tense — 'they break apart / they may come apart'. The structure expresses that because of fear and grief they can be undone at any moment.
segments.s236-g81: اگر introduces a conditional ('if'). سالیان is a temporal/durational expression ('for years') placed before the affected nouns. رنج و رزم are coordinated direct objects meaning 'toil/hardship and battle'. آوری is a 2nd-person singular present/present-subjunctive form of آوردن ('you bring/you endure/you cause'); here it links the conditional clause to the following result clause.
segments.s236-g82: نباشد is an impersonal negative present/jussive ('there is not / will not be / let there not be'); it functions as the main verb of the result clause. بدستت literally 'in your hand' (بدست + enclitic ت) means 'you have' or 'is at your disposal'. جز از introduces the exception 'except from / except by', and داوری ('judgment') is the exception. Note the poetic inversion where the negative verb precedes the phrase بدستت.
segments.s236-g83: نیاید is a negative jussive/optative ('should not come / must not be'); it is impersonal and here functions like 'let it not be'. برین باره is 'on this burden' or 'this load' (بر + این → برین, باره = 'load/burden'), and بر منجنیق places that load 'upon the catapult' (منجنیق = siege engine). Word order is verb first (poetic) followed by the affected phrase.
segments.s236-g84: از introduces the instrumental/cause ('by/through' or 'because of'). افسون means 'sorcery/charm'. سلم here is a lexical item whose listed senses (ladder / salutation) do not neatly fit the context (likely a proper name or title in the poem) — its sense is therefore marked as uncertain. و links the two causes, دم is 'breath' (here figuratively 'utterance/word' or 'influence'), and جاثلیق is the title 'Catholicos' (a senior cleric) — together 'by the sorcery of Salm and the breath of the Catholicos.'
segments.s236-g85: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. بشنید is the simple past 'he heard'. رستم is the subject following the verb (poetic order). پر اندیشه شد is a predicate: پر 'full (of)' + اندیشه 'thought', plus شد 'became' — together 'became full of thought.'
segments.s236-g86: دلش is 'his heart' (دل + enclitic ش). از غم و درد is a cause phrase 'from sorrow and pain' (از = from/because of). چون بیشه شد is a simile: چون 'like' + بیشه 'a thicket/grove' + شد 'became' — 'became like a thicket' (a poetic image for a heart full of turmoil).
segments.s236-g87: یکی here is 'one / a single' or 'one person' used to introduce a single action. رزم کرد is a compound meaning 'fought a battle' (رزم = battle, کرد = did/made → 'he fought'). آن نه بر آرزوی is a negative clause fragment 'that not by the wish' (آن نه = 'that not', بر آرزوی = 'by the desire/wish of'); in context it says the fighting was not done at the army's desire.
segments.s236-g88: سپاه اندر آورد is 'he brought/led the army in' (سپاه = host/army, اندر = into/among, آورد = brought/led). بر چار سوی is a directional phrase 'to the four sides/quarters' (چار = 'four', سوی = 'direction/side'), meaning he deployed or sent the host out in all four directions. The past verb آورد agrees with the earlier action.
segments.s236-g137: چو is the temporal subordinating word 'when' introducing a past clause. از پاک یزدان is a prepositional phrase (from/to the Pure God) serving as the object/target of the verb; پاک is an adjectival modifier before the proper noun یزدان. بپرداختند is past, 3rd‑person plural; here it means 'they directed/attended (themselves) to' or 'they addressed/paid (honour) to' the Divine.
segments.s236-g138: بران is an adverbial/prepositional form 'upon/unto that' pointing back to the addressee; نامدار is an adjective 'renowned' qualifying the implied person; آفرین is a noun meaning 'acclamation/praise' and functions as the direct object; ساختند is past 3rd‑person plural 'they made/formed' and together آفرین ساختند is a fixed expression 'they gave/uttered praise (acclamation)'.
segments.s236-g139: که begins an explanatory/relative clause ('for/that'). هر کس که is the common relative 'whoever/anyone who'; the second که is the relative particle linking the following descriptive clause. چون تو نباشد بجنگ is a subordinate predicate: چون 'like/as', تو 'you' and نباشد is a negative present/subjunctive form ('is not' / 'be not'), while بجنگ is the verbal stem 'to fight' (here indicating fighting ability or participation). The clause therefore says 'whoever is not like you in fighting.'
segments.s236-g140: نشستن is the verbal noun 'sitting' (here 'sitting out' of combat) used as the subject/thing under discussion. به آید is an impersonal/ethical construction 'it comes to / it befits' (آید third‑person singular present), so نشستن به آید = 'it befits/as for sitting'. بنام و بننگ are two coordinated nouns 'with name/fame and with disgrace/shame' (i.e. resulting reputation); the phrase describes the reputational consequence of sitting out the fight.
segments.s236-g141: This is a coordinate NP describing the addressee's attributes. تن 'body' + پیل 'elephant' (figurative 'elephantine body') followed by داری, present 2nd‑person singular 'you have'. The second coordinated noun phrase چنگال شیر 'the claws of a lion' is joined by و 'and'. The word order is [possessed things] + داری (you have).
segments.s236-g142: زمانی here is temporal ('at any time' / 'ever' / 'for a while' depending on nuance). نباشی is 2nd‑person negative subjunctive/jussive used as a wish/optative 'may you not be' or 'do not be'. ز is the preposition 'from', پیگار is the noun 'battle', and سیر is 'sated/satiated'; together ز پیگار سیر means 'sated with battle'. The whole line is a wish: 'may you never be sated by fighting.'
segments.s236-g143: تهمتن is the speaker's name/epithet (Tahmātan). چنین گفت is the reporting formula 'said thus' (چنین 'thus', گفت used here as 'said'). کین is an archaic/demonstrative particle meaning 'this' (classical usage) pointing to the following noun phrase; زور و فر are two coordinated nouns 'strength (زور) and glory/splendour (فر)'. The phrase introduces Tahmatan's statement: 'this strength and glory...'.
segments.s236-g144: یکی is the numeral/adjective 'one' emphasizing unity; خلعتی is a noun 'robe (bestowed)' or 'a bestowed cloak' and is the head noun of the phrase. باشد is 3rd‑person singular present/subjunctive of بودن used here to state existence/identity ('is/is like'). از دادگر is a genitive/agentive phrase 'from the Just (One)' — i.e., granted by the Just (God). The full clause means 'they (both) are one robe granted by the Just.'
segments.s236-g313: This clause begins with the negator نه governing a verb: برتابد is a present/subjunctive-type form meaning "to endure/withstand." آهنگ here is used in the sense of a forceful motion or onslaught (not "melody"). او is the third-person pronoun "he/his." The phrase ژنده پیل is a compound (ژنده "tattered") modifying پیل "elephant." The whole clause means "not even a tattered elephant endures his onset."
segments.s236-g314: This is the second coordinate of a symmetric نه ... نه ... negation (elliptical: the same predicate of endurance is understood). کشتی سلیحش functions as a noun phrase "the ship of his armament/armed ship" (سلیح + possessive -ش). بدریای نیل here names the sea/area (poetic phrase) — the clause asserts that even that ship cannot withstand/face the Nile-sea; the verbal action is omitted but carried over from the first conjunct.
segments.s236-g315: یکی is an indefinite "a/certain" introducing a single exemplifying image. کوه زیرش is a noun phrase "a mountain under him/beneath him." بکردار is used adjectivally/predicatively (here meaning roughly "shaped/of form" in the poem; the supplied senses approximate this as "well‑behaved/well‑formed"), and باد at the end is the optative/assumptive particle (poetic use) meaning roughly "as if/one might say" (literally "may be/would be"), so the clause reads like an imagistic assertion: "a mountain under him, (as if) well‑formed by the wind."
segments.s236-g316: تو گویی is an idiomatic evaluative phrase: literally "you would say" (used impersonally to mean "one would think/it seems as if"). که introduces the subordinate clause "that ..."; از باد دارد نژاد is a simple clause: از = "from/of," باد = "the wind," دارد = present/has (possession/state), نژاد = "origin/lineage/birth." Together: "you would say that he has his origin from the wind."
segments.s236-g317: This coordinate phrase lists two possessed qualities with دارد "has/possesses." تگ آهوان is a poetic comparison indicating swiftness/grace associated with ahvān (gazelles); و links the next noun phrase هول شیر "the terror/dread of a lion." Thus the structure is "he has X and Y." (Note: تگ is a poetic term here whose precise athletic meaning isn't one of the supplied senses.)
segments.s236-g318: بناورد is an archaic/transitive verb here meaning something like "meets/encounters/engages with" or "is brought into contact with." With the prepositional phrase با شیر "with a lion," it forms the conditional/occasional clause; گردد is the present/future‑oriented verb "become," and دلیر is the predicate adjective "brave." Together: "(when) he meets a lion, he becomes brave."
segments.s236-g319: سخن گوید is a nominal + verb phrase "(he) speaks/will speak." ار is the conditional "if." زو = از + او (poetic) "of/from him" or "to him" and کنی is the second‑person present subjunctive/imperative "you make/do"; خواستار is used as a noun/adjective "a petitioner/one who asks." The clause means roughly "he will speak if you become a petitioner to him / if you ask him as a supplicant."
segments.s236-g320: This short clause is temporal/comparative: بدریا here is a poetic adverbial meaning "to/into the sea" (or 'at sea'); چو = "when/as" introduces the temporal clause; کشتی بود = "the ship was" (بود = past of to be); روز کار = "a day of action/work" (روز 'day', کار 'affair/task'). Together it gives a situational note: "when his ship was at sea, it was a day of action."
segments.s236-g153: بشد is a narrative past/auxiliary meaning 'it happened' or 'began' here introducing the event; the phrase follows a classical word order where the subject (گیو) follows the verb. با آن سواران is a prepositional phrase 'with those horsemen' (با + demonstrative آن + plural noun), and جنگ stands as the event/object 'battle/war' that Giv entered into.
segments.s236-g154: سه روز is a temporal phrase 'three days' (duration). اندر آن is a locative/temporal classical prepositional phrase 'in/within that (event)'. تاختن is a verbal noun (masdar) 'the charging/ride/attack' used as a noun-like event; شد is a past verb meaning 'occurred/happened' and takes درنگ 'pause' as its complement, so the clause literally says 'a pause happened in that charging for three days.'
segments.s236-g155: بدانگه که is a temporal/conjunctive phrase 'thereupon/when', introducing a subordinate clause. خورشید is the subject 'the sun' and بنمود is a past verb 'showed/appeared' taking تاج 'the crown' as its object—classical narrative order where the conjunction opens the clause and the verb follows the subject.
segments.s236-g156: برآمد and نشست are two consecutive past verbs: برآمد 'arose/rose' (came up) followed by نشست 'sat' (past of نشستن). The prepositional sequence از بر تخت عاج literally 'from/on the ivory throne' is an older/pre-classical way to indicate the surface/location—here rendering 'sat upon the ivory throne.'
segments.s236-g157: ز توران is the source prepositional phrase 'from Turan' (ز is the poetic form of از). بیامد is the past tense 'came'; the adjective سرافراز follows the verb and qualifies گیو, yielding the inversional narrative order 'from Turan came triumphant Giv.'
segments.s236-g158: گرفته is a past participle 'having taken/seized' modifying an implied object list that follows; بسی is a poetic quantifier 'many/much'; نامداران is a plural noun 'renowned ones/illustrious persons'; نیو here is an instrument name (ney, the reed‑flute) and so part of the list of items taken.
