Grammatically this is a compact list introduced by the participle گرفته.
segments.s236-g159: بسی is the quantifier 'many.' خوب‌چهره is a compound adjective 'fair‑faced/beautiful‑faced.' بتان here is used poetically for 'beautiful women/beleoveds' (idol‑like beauties), and طراز is a modifier meaning 'in the style/form of' so the sequence forms a descriptive noun phrase 'many fair‑faced, idol‑fashioned beauties.'
segments.s236-g160: گرانمایه is an adjective 'precious/esteemed' modifying اسپان 'horses/steeds.' و is the coordinating 'and.' هرگونه is a distributive/qualifying phrase 'every kind of' modifying ساز 'instrument' (saz), so the phrase lists valuable horses and all sorts of musical instruments.
segments.s236-g289: سرانجام is an adverb meaning 'finally' placed at the clause front; رستم is the subject (proper name). بخم کمند is an old-fashioned noun phrase in which بخم is an archaic word meaning the coil/loop and کمند is 'lasso' — together 'the coil of the lasso.' The verb is not in this segment but will follow in the next segment, so this segment supplies subject and object before the verb.
segments.s236-g290: This is the verb phrase. ز پیل اندر آورد is a compound expression: ز = 'from', پیل = 'elephant', اندر آورد = 'brought out/ drew out' (آورد is past 3rd sing.). The conjunction و links the next verb; بنهاد is an archaic past form of نهادن meaning 'placed/put' (here 'fastened/laid on'), and بند is the object 'fetter, bond'. Both actions share the subject (رستم) from the previous segment.
segments.s236-g291: A noun phrase listing two coordinated groups: سواران 'riders' and گردان 'regiments/troops'. هر کشوری is a postposed genitive-like modifier meaning 'of every country/realm' (هر qualifies کشور). The conjunction و simply coordinates the two nouns.
segments.s236-g292: ز هر سو که بود is a relative/circumstantial clause 'from every quarter that there was' (که introduces the relative idea; بود is past 3rd sing. 'was'). از بزرگان سری follows and means 'from the great/leading men' where از = 'from/of' and سری here = 'heads/chiefs' (i.e. foremost nobles). The clause explains the origin/composition of the riders and regiments.
segments.s236-g293: بدین کشور آمد is classical fusion of به + این ('to/into this') + کشور ('country') + آمد (past 'came'), so 'came into this country.' کنون 'now' is a temporal adverb. زین نشان is likewise classical/beated language meaning 'by this sign/token' (زین = 'by this' in classical usage; here it frames the coming). Note that the tokenizer split بدین and زین into parts; read them as the classical phrases 'به این' and 'ز این نشان'.
segments.s236-g294: همان is a demonstrative meaning 'those same' or 'that very'; تاجداران is plural 'crown-bearers' or 'sovereigns'; گردنکشان is a descriptive plural 'neck-raisers' literally, i.e. proud/arrogant ones — here used in the negative sense 'rebels/proud ones'. The three words form a noun phrase identifying who came.
segments.s236-g295: من ایدر نمانم is 'I will not remain here' in classical Persian: ایدر = ایـن‌جا (here) and نمانم is 1st singular negative/form of ماندن (I do not stay / I will not remain). بسی گنج و تخت is a nominative/absolutive noun phrase 'much/ample treasure and throne' giving the contrast or available wealth. The two parts form a short antithetical statement: 'I will not stay here; [there is] much treasure and a throne.'
segments.s236-g296: که introduces a causal/explanatory clause here ('for/because'). گردان is 'the host/troop' as subject; شدست is an archaic/periphrastic past form ('has become' or 'has been made/put'), with the enclitic -ست marking 3rd person. اندرین کار سخت is the locative/oblique phrase 'in/into this hard affair' (اندرین = 'in this/these', کار = 'affair/task', سخت = 'difficult'). Together: 'for the host has been put to this hard task.'
segments.s236-g265: 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from' placed before the noun phrase; 'گردان پهلومنش' is a noun + postpositive adjective ('گردان' = troop/host, 'پهلومنش' = champion-like/valiant) in typical Persian adjective-after-noun order. 'چند مرد' follows as an interrogative/indefinite quantifier + head noun ('چند' = how many/several before 'مرد'), so the whole segment supplies the base noun phrase 'from the troop of champions, how many men'.
segments.s236-g266: 'که' introduces a relative/subordinate clause linking to the preceding noun phrase ('who/that'); 'آورد سازند' is a two‑verb classical/periphrastic expression where 'آورد' (bring/cause) combines with the following verb 'سازند' (3rd pl. present/subjunctive/optative form of ساختن) to mean 'would bring about / cause to come'; 'روز نبرد' is a genitive compound ('day of battle'), with روز (day) + نبرد (battle). The clause thus specifies those men as ones who would bring about the battle‑day.
segments.s236-g267: 'چو' is the temporal conjunction 'when'; 'فرغار' is used here as a proper name; 'برگشت' and 'آمد' are simple past verbs (3rd sg.) meaning 'returned' and 'came' respectively, connected by 'و' ('and/then'). 'براه' is an older fused prepositional phrase (به راه) meaning 'onto/the road' or 'on the way', so the whole segment reads 'when Farghār returned and came on the road.'
segments.s236-g268: 'بکارآگهی شد' functions like a passive/state expression with 'شد' as the auxiliary ('was made/was put'), where 'بکارآگهی' acts as a verbal/adjectival phrase meaning 'called into use / put on notice' (poetic/archaic). 'سپاه' is the noun 'army' and appears after 'بایران' (here a problematic/uncertain lexical item in the given lemma list); overall word order is verb/adjectival phrase + (possible locative/target) + subject 'سپاه' yielding 'the army was put on alert.'
segments.s236-g269: This is a verbal clause where 'غمی شد' = 'a grief/sorrow came to be' (غمی = grief as subject, شد = became/came to pass), followed by the possessee 'دل' (heart) and the possessor/description 'مرد پرخاشجوی' (the quarrelsome/aggressive man). Together it means 'sorrow seized/fell upon the heart of the quarrelsome man.'
segments.s236-g270: 'ببیگانگان' is an older/prepositional form equivalent to 'به بیگانگان' ('to/for strangers' or 'before strangers'); 'ایچ' is an archaic negating adverb meaning 'nothing / not at all'; 'ننمود' is the negative past form of 'نمود' (to show/appear) — here 'he did not show' — and 'روی' is 'face'. The word order places the indirect object/phrase first for emphasis: 'to strangers he showed his face not at all.'
segments.s236-g271: 'فرستاد' is a simple past transitive verb 'he sent'; 'و' links actions; 'فرزند را' is the definite object marked by 'را' ('his son' as the object); 'پیش خواند' is a compound verb meaning 'called before/in his presence' (پیش = 'before/in presence of', خواند here = 'called/summoned' rather than 'read'), so the clause means 'he sent and summoned his son forward.'
segments.s236-g272: 'بسی راز بایسته' is a noun phrase where 'بسی' is a classical intensifier ('many/much'); 'راز' is the head noun 'secrets' and 'بایسته' is a postpositive adjective 'necessary/appropriate.' 'با او' ('with him') marks the recipient/companion in older usage (here: 'to him' / 'with him'), and 'براند' is a 3rd‑person verbal form used here in the sense 'he gave/imparted / told' (archaic usage). The whole segment thus says he related/imparted many necessary secrets to him.
segments.s236-g1: وزان (poetic compound of و + آن) is a deictic linking particle 'from that / and from that'; جایگه is a noun 'place'. The verb phrase لشکر اندر کشید literally places the object before the verb in older/poetic word order: لشکر (the army) + اندر (a locative/prepositional particle 'into / in') + کشید (past tense). Here کشید functions as a movement verb 'drew/moved' (classical usage 'to draw up/move [troops]'), so the clause means 'from that place he drew up (moved) the army.'
segments.s236-g2: بیک منزلی is a contracted poetic expression for به یک منزلی ('at/after one stage or halt'); here منزلی is used in the sense 'station / stage' rather than 'domestic'. بر یکی شهر دید places the object after the preposition: بر + یکی شهر + دید — i.e. 'he saw a certain city before/upon him.' دید is simple past 'he saw.'
segments.s236-g3: This is a nominal identification: 'نام آن شهر بیداد بود' = 'the name of that city was Bīdād.' کجا here opens the line in a poetic/archaic way (roughly 'where' or a formulaic introducer) but does not function as a modern interrogative. بود is past 'was.'
segments.s236-g4: دژی بود is simple past 'it was a fortress.' وز مردم آباد بود is a compressed poetic clause: وز here stands for وِ ز (و + از) or a linking 'and (of)'; مردم آباد بود = 'the people were prosperous/settled.' The two clauses are coordinated and describe the city's character and condition.
segments.s236-g5: همه خوردنیشان is 'all their eatables / all their food' (خوردنی + شان). ز مردم marks the source 'from the people.' بدی here denotes the manner 'by wrongdoing / oppression' — the clause says they obtained their sustenance from the populace by unjust means. Word order is topic-initial (همه) then possessive noun and source.
segments.s236-g6: پری چهره‌ای is a descriptive phrase 'fairy‑faced / with a fairy's face' (an adjective phrase). هر زمان 'at every time / always' is an adverbial phrase modifying the following predicate; گم بدی is a compressed poetic pairing meaning they were habitually wicked/astray — بدی is the substantive 'wickedness/evil', while گم is an adjective/participial sense 'astray/losing the right path' (poetic), so together they portray continual vice.
segments.s236-g7: بخوان is an imperative/hortative 'read/consider / know' used by the narrator to introduce a judgment. چنان شهریار پلید is a noun phrase 'such a vile king/monarch' (چنان = 'such'); the whole segment is an instruction to regard the following statement.
segments.s236-g8: نبودی is past negative of بودن 'was not.' جز از introduces an exception or limiting phrase 'except from / nothing but.' کودک نارسید is a noun phrase 'an unripe/immature child' (نارسید here adjectival 'unripe / not matured'). Together the clause says he was not truly the vile sovereign — he was merely an under‑grown child.
segments.s236-g481: This is an address/noun phrase with an omitted verb (typical of epic Persian imperatives or hortatory statements): 'تو' is the vocative/second‑person pronoun, followed by 'لشکر برآغال' (‘the army wearing/with head‑bands’), and the prepositional phrase 'بر لشکرش' ('upon/against his army'). Word order places the addressee first for emphasis; the action verb is supplied by the next segment.
segments.s236-g482: 'بانبوه' is an adverbial phrase 'in great numbers'; 'تا' introduces the purposive/result clause here ('so that' / 'until'); 'خیره گردد' is a two‑word predicate: 'خیره' (dazed/astonished) plus the present/subjunctive/optative 'گردد' meaning 'become' — together 'becomes dazed'; 'سرش' = 'his head' (سر + enclitic possessive ش). The clause gives the intended result of the previous injunction.
segments.s236-g483: 'مگر' here functions as a conditional negative 'unless'; 'چاره' = 'remedy/plan'; 'سازم' is first‑person present/subjunctive of ساختن ('I may/should make' or 'I contrive'); 'وگر' = 'and if' (introducing the alternative); 'نی' is the negation 'not'; 'بدست' ('by/through the hand') functions adverbially 'by hand' or 'by myself'. The whole segment sets up a conditional: unless I can contrive a plan, and if not by my own hand... .
segments.s236-g484: 'بر و یال' is a coordinated noun phrase 'brow and mane' (the first element 'بر' here functions as a nominal element in poetic diction and is linked by 'و' to 'یال'); 'او را' marks possession/object ('his ...' with the object marker 'را' for emphasis); 'نشاید' is an impersonal/formal negative modal 'it is not fitting/possible'; 'شکست' ('break') here is the action that 'نشاید' negates — together: 'it is not possible to break his brow and mane.'
segments.s236-g485: 'ازو' is a prepositional phrase 'because of him / on account of him'; 'شاد شد' is the two‑word verb meaning 'became glad / rejoiced' (شاد + شد); 'جان افراسیاب' ('the life/soul of Afrasiab') is the patient/experiencer whose joy is described — classical Persian often places the oblique phrase before the subject for emphasis ('Because of him, Afrasiab's soul rejoiced').
segments.s236-g486: 'می‌روشن آورد' literally reads as 'brought (the) shining ones' — here 'می‌روشن' (bright/shining) functions as a descriptor of lights/torches and with 'آورد' (past 'brought') yields 'he brought lights/torches'; the following 'و چنگ و رباب' lists musical instruments ('and a chang [harp] and a rebab'). 'می‌' + adjective used nominally is a poetic device to mean 'the shining (things)'.
segments.s236-g487: 'بدانگه' = 'at that moment / thereupon'; the conjunction 'که' here introduces the subordinate clause 'that/when'; 'شد مست' = 'became drunk/intoxicated' (شد as auxiliary/perfective with the adjective مست); 'پولادوند' is the epithet/name of the man ('Pouladvand' = 'steel‑hearted/steel‑clad'). The clause gives the temporal circumstance for the speech that follows.
segments.s236-g488: 'چنین گفت' = 'thus he said' (چنین 'thus/so' + گفت past of 'to say'); 'با او' = 'to him/with him' (با used as 'to' or 'with' in reported speech); 'ببانگ بلند' is an adverbial phrase 'with a loud cry' (ب‑ prefixed prepositional/adverbial form 'with/by' + 'بانگ' 'a cry' + 'بلند' 'loud'). The whole segment reports the utterance and its manner.
segments.s236-g41: Poetic/inverted word order: the verb phrase 'گران کرد' (made heavy/severe) appears before its subject 'رستم'. 'گران' is a predicative adjective used as object/complement meaning 'grave/weighty' and 'کرد' is the past-tense perfective 'made/did'. 'زمانی' is an adverbial 'for a while', and 'رکیب' (here 'rider/companion' or rival) is the affected person described by that adverbial phrase.
segments.s236-g42: 'ندانست' is the simple past negative of 'دانستن' ('did not know'); the following noun 'لشکر' is the subject after the verb (inverted order). 'فراز از نشیب' is a fixed-type contrast 'ups from downs' (فراز = top, از = from, نشیب = descent), so the clause says the army could not distinguish/know high from low.
segments.s236-g43: 'بدانسان' is an adverb meaning 'in that way/thus'. 'بیامد' is the past tense 'he came'. The phrase 'بدان رزمگاه' is composed of the prefix ب- ('to/into') plus a demonstrative/marker and the noun 'رزمگاه' ('battlefield'), so the full chunk means 'he came to that battlefield.' Note that the annotation split 'بدان' into 'ب' and 'دان' but here 'بدان' functions as 'to that'.
segments.s236-g44: The conjunction 'که' introduces a relative/clausal comment 'where/that'. 'باد' is the subject 'the wind' (not the optative particle here). 'اندر آید' is a present/habitual or vivid narrative 'comes in', with 'اندر' = 'in/into'. 'ز کوه سیاه' means 'from the Black Mountain' (ز = from, کوه = mountain, سیاه = black/dark).
segments.s236-g45: 'فراوان' is an adverb/adjective 'abundant/many' placed before the noun phrase. 'ز ایرانیان کشته' is a genitive/partitive phrase 'slain from the Iranians' (i.e. Iranians who were killed). 'دید' is the past-tense verb 'he saw' concluding the clause.
segments.s236-g46: 'بسی' here means 'many/a great number' (an intensifier) followed by the noun 'سرکش' ('unruly/rebellious/wild ones'). The phrase 'از جنگ برگشته' is a participial phrase 'having returned from the war' that modifies 'سرکش'. 'دید' is again the past 'he saw.'
segments.s236-g47: 'بکافور' is presented as the speaker (here a proper name or epithet); the supplied sense list does not match that usage so it is left unspecified. 'گفت' in narrative is 'said' (past). 'ای سگ بدگهر' is a vocative phrase: 'O dog, evil-tempered (scoundrel)' — 'ای' marks the address, 'سگ' is an insulting vocative, and 'بدگهر' qualifies him as bad/evil-minded.
segments.s236-g48: 'کنون' = 'now'. The coordinated noun phrase 'رزم و رنج' = 'battle and hardship/suffering' is the thing that 'آمد بسر' (has 'come upon' someone). 'تو' is the vocative/second-person 'you'. 'آمد' is past 'has come' and the idiom 'آمد بسر' means 'befell/has come upon' (i.e. 'now battle and suffering have befallen you').
segments.s236-g369: یکی is the indefinite 'a certain' (not the numeral); بور is an adjective 'fair‑haired' placed after it as in epic diction; ابرش ('his cloud' / 'dark brow') is a descriptive noun with the possessive -ش attached to refer back to the man. به پیشش is a prepositional phrase 'before him / in front of him' (به + پیش + pronominal clitic), and بپای is a verbal form 'stood / lay in wait' (b‑ prefix + verb root) functioning as the main verb of the clause.
segments.s236-g370: تو is the 2nd‑person pronoun 'you' (poetic/thou); گفتی here functions as a reported‑speech verb 'you said' (past) even though the lexical entry shows noun senses — in the line it marks reported utterance. همی is the emphatic/progressive particle 'just/now/soon' attaching to the following verb; اندر آید is a periphrastic present/future 'will come forth' (اندر + آید = 'come into view'), ز جای 'from his place' gives the origin (ز = from, جای = place).
segments.s236-g371: سپهدار is a title 'the commander' or 'general' serving as the head noun. چون here functions as the comparative/illustrative 'such as / like' that introduces a list of exemplars; the following items طوس و گودرز و گیو are proper names connected by و ('and'), so the noun + چون phrase lists prominent commanders.
segments.s236-g372: This is a continuation of the list introduced by چون: فریبرز و شیدوش و گرگین نیو are names joined by the coordinating و. The sequence is nominal (no verb here) — a typical epic catalogue where names are simply enumerated.
segments.s236-g373: طلایه 'vanguard' is the subject noun; گرازست is a nominal/cognate predicate ('is Gorgaz' or 'is a boar‑like one' depending on reading), where -ست is the copular suffix 'is'. با گستهم is a prepositional phrase 'with Gostaham' (با + proper name) that attaches to and modifies who composes the vanguard.
segments.s236-g374: که introduces a subordinate/relative clause linking to the previous statement ('and/which'), here meaning roughly 'and that/and so'. با + two names بیژن گیو expresses accompaniment 'with Bijan and Giv'; باشد is the present/subjunctive copula 'is / may be' used here as a simple existential/predicative 'are', and بهم ('beham') means 'together'.
segments.s236-g375: غمی شد is an idiomatic past construction 'grief arose / he was grieved' (غمی 'grief' + شد 'became/happened'). ز گفتار is the ablative 'because of / from the speech of', and فرغار شاه is a name + title (Farghār the king) in a genitive relation to گفتار ('the speech of Farghār the king').
segments.s236-g376: کس آمد is 'someone came' (کس = someone, آمد = came). بر is the preposition 'to / toward', and پهلوان سپاه is a genitival/constructional phrase 'the champion of the army' (پهلوان 'champion' governing سپاه 'army'), so the whole clause means 'someone came to the army's champion.'
segments.s236-g33: فراوان is a quantifier placed before the phrase and means 'many' or 'abundantly'. ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/of', so فراوان ز ایرانیان = 'many of the Iranians'. کشته شد is a passive construction: کشته is the past participle 'killed/slain' and شد functions as the auxiliary forming the passive 'were slain'.
segments.s236-g34: بسر is used in the sense 'to live / to spend (one's life)'; بر + سپهر بلا is a prepositional phrase 'upon/under the sky of calamity' (سپهر = sky; بلا = calamity). گشته is the participle 'become/turned' and شد again is the auxiliary, so گشته شد yields 'was turned / came to be'. The whole phrase means that living/existence was cast under a sky of affliction.
segments.s236-g35: ببیژن is a dative/address form 'to Bijan' (or 'O Bijan' in direct address). چنین گفت = 'said thus' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = 'said' in reported speech). گستهم is a first-person perfect/preterit 'I wandered' (reported speech inside the clause) and زود is an adverb 'soon/quickly' modifying it.
segments.s236-g36: که introduces the subordinate clause 'that'. لختی is a temporal phrase 'for a little while'. عنانت is 'your reins' (عنان + enclitic -ت), with عنان used literal as 'reins/bridle'. بباید is the modal 'must/ought to' (present/future necessity), and بسود is an adverbial phrase 'to advantage / for profit'. Word order places the time phrase before the object and modal.
segments.s236-g37: برستم functions here as the indirect object 'to Rostam' (tell it to Rostam). بگویی is the reporting verb 'you should say / tell' in subordinate style, and که introduces the content 'that / how many'. چندین is a quantifier 'several / many a', and مایست is an archaic/poetic form meaning roughly 'must stand' or 'must remain' (an older verbal/obligative form).
segments.s236-g38: بجنبان is an imperative or causative imperative 'shake! / set (it) moving' directed at the reins (the verb takes عنان as its object). عنان is the direct object 'reins', با introduces an accompaniment 'with', and سواری دویست (poetic inversion here) means 'two hundred riders/horsemen' — سواری = rider. The whole clause orders to put the reins in motion with two hundred mounted men.
segments.s236-g39: بشد here is an optative 'may it be / let it be' introducing a wish or blessing. The sequence بیژن گیو برسان is an optative/imperative clause 'let Bijan bring/deliver Giv' (بیژن = agent, گیو = the person brought, برسان = 'bring/deliver'). باد at the end is an optative particle reinforcing the wish ('may it be so').
segments.s236-g40: سخن = 'the word/news/report' is the theme; بر تهمتن = 'to Tahmatan' (بر used here as 'to/toward' plus the proper name تهمتن). همه is the agent 'all / everyone', کرد is the past 'did/made', and یاد is 'remembrance / reminder' — together کرد یاد = 'caused a reminder' i.e. 'reminded'. So the clause says that everyone reminded Tahmatan of the news.
segments.s236-g345: تو is the vocative/2nd-person pronoun addressing someone (’you’/’thou’). بر باد here participates in the idiom بر باد رفتن ‘to be ruined / go to the wind’ (بر + باد literally ‘to the wind’), and این جنگ is the object (‘this war’). کشتی مران is a noun phrase ‘the ship of Maran’ (مرَان read as a proper name in vocative context); word order is poetic (object phrases can appear before or after the idiom).
segments.s236-g346: چو here = ‘when’ (introducing a temporal clause). دانی is 2nd-person present of دانستن (‘you know’). که introduces the subordinate clause ‘that …’. آمد is a past (perfect) verb ‘came / has come’. سپاهی گران is a noun phrase ‘a heavy / mighty army’ (سپاهی = host/army, گران = weighty/powerful) completing the content of the subordinate clause.
segments.s236-g347: ز is the poetic preposition ‘from / among’. شاهان گیتی is a genitive phrase ‘kings of the world’ (شاهان = kings, گیتی = world). گزیده is a past participle/adjective ‘chosen / selected’ describing the addressee. توی at the end functions as a locative/emphatic particle meaning ‘among / in’ (poetic order: ‘chosen among the kings of the world are you’).
segments.s236-g348: جهانجوی is a compound adjective/noun ‘world‑seeking’ (an ambitious person seeking dominion). و is the coordinating conjunction ‘and’. هم is an adverb ‘also / too’ (here ‘and also’). کار دیده is a compound where کار = ‘affairs / deeds’ and دیده is the past participle ‘seen’ = ‘experienced’ (i.e. experienced in deeds). توی here functions like ‘in/within you’ (poetic: ‘world‑seeking and experienced in affairs are in you’).
segments.s236-g349: بجان و سر is a fixed pairing meaning ‘life and head’ (literally ‘by life and head’, here idiomatically ‘lives and leaders’ / ‘life and person’). شاه توران سپاه is a poetic word order for سپاهِ شاهِ توران ‘the army of the King of Turan’ (genitive chain: army of the king of Turan). The whole fragment nominates the army and what it takes/affects.
segments.s236-g350: بخورشید is the prepositional contraction به خورشید (‘to/against the sun’) or read as a poetic clustering meaning ‘(it) to the sun’ — in context the phrase lists grand things taken or overwhelmed (خورشید = sun). و ماه و بتخت و کلاه continues the list ‘and moon, and throne, and crown/helmet’ (بتخت here is a contracted/poetic form read as تخت ‘throne’; کلاه often = hat/helmet, here crown/helmet). The line is idiomatic: the army ‘devours/overwhelms’ these splendours.
segments.s236-g351: که introduces an explanatory clause ‘that / because’. از is the preposition ‘from / because of’. کار governs the following names: کاموس و خاقانِ چین — two agents whose deeds are blamed. کاموس here seems to be a proper name (not captured by the provided senses), and خاقان چین is ‘the Khagan of China’ (خاقان = khagan, چین = China). The phrase means ‘because of the deeds of Kamus and the Chinese khagan’.
segments.s236-g352: دلم گشت is the common poetic expression: دل + ـم (possessive ‘my’) + گشت (become) = ‘my heart became…’. پر خون = ‘full of blood’ (پر = full of, خون = blood; blood here = bloodshed/grief). و سر پر ز کین = ‘and (my) head full of kīn (vengeance/hatred)’; word order places the possessed parts before predicates in a compressed, emphatic style.
